item.title: Why Jayco Aren't Racing for GC with Gene Bates
item.description: Gene Bates, Sporting Manager at Jayco AlUla, joins the Domestique Hotseat with Aidan Burgess on the eve of the Tour de France in Barcelona. They discuss the team's plan to target stage wins rather than ride for the general classification, Michael Matthews' return from two broken wrists, Mauro Schmid's move to Q36.5, and why Bates believes nobody can stop Pogačar this July. He also talks about managing Ben O'Connor after a huge 2024, how Jayco preserves its Australian identity while becoming a truly international team, the stage 1 team time trial, and why they would rather support a departing rider than leave him at home.

item.title: Can Anyone Stop Tadej Pogačar? | Tour de France 2026 Preview
item.description: Lance, Johan, George, Sir Bradley, and Spencer Martin break down the 2026 Tour de France, which begins tomorrow in Barcelona with a team time trial. They discuss the return of the TTT, how it could shape the overall battle, and what it means for team tactics and internal dynamics. They also look ahead to another GC showdown between Jonas Vingegaard and Tadej Pogačar, whether younger riders like Paul Seixas or Isaac del Toro can disrupt the duopoly, and what it would mean for France to finally have a top GC contender in its home race. item.title: S14 Ep76: KM0: It’s Coming Home
item.description: This episode is available ad-free on our Friends of the Podcast feed. Subscribe at thecyclingpodcast.com. "I wouldn't have bet a single centime on a Frenchman winning the Tour for forty years," said Bernard Hinault, who described it as an "unwanted record" as the fourth decade since his 1985 victory was about to end last July. "We haven't got any chance…" Hinault added in the same interview. Kevin Vauquelin's brave seventh place on GC aside, it seemed unthinkable after three weeks that the drought would not stretch to forty-one years in 2026, then forty-two and beyond, at least until Tadej Pogačar either began a steep decline or retired. Yet the idea of Paul Seixas winning the 2026 race at age 19 would also seem unthinkable to many long-time observers. It would certainly be historic, not only because Seixas would bring the Tour home for the first time since Hinault in 1985, but also because he would be the youngest winner ever: 49 days younger than Henri Cornet, who won the Tour's second edition in 1904. It sounds improbable, and logic suggests the same — and yet this summer, France expects, hopes, and to some extent believes. And if, after the first skirmishes in the Pyrenees in week one, the boy from Beaujolais has not been Pogcinerated out of contention, the excitement will be enormous. Having witnessed Seixas's definitive coming of age at the Tour of the Basque Country in April, Daniel Friebe will follow what could be the story of the summer at the Tour de France over three weeks in July. Not necessarily by tracking Seixas's every pedal stroke, but by exploring how France got here — now nearing half a century into one of professional sport's most painful and baffling droughts — and how, one day, whether soon or still uncomfortably far away, deliverance may finally arrive. The 2026 Tour begins in Barcelona, and so does our series, outside a curious hospital building in the heart of the Catalan capital that, twenty years ago, was somehow tasked with shaping the future of professional cycling. In episode one, Daniel explains exactly what happened, and why it determined the destiny of Paul Seixas.

item.title: Behind the Scenes at the Tour de France
item.description: The Tour de France is here, and For The Love Of Cycling is on the ground. In this special episode, Ned Boulting, Lizzie Deignan, and Pete Kennaugh record from inside the XDS-Astana team bus on the eve of Stage 1, taking you right into the heart of the biggest race in cycling. With Pete now working as a DS for Astana, the team offers a rare behind-the-scenes look at the atmosphere inside a Tour de France setup before racing begins, as they catch up, discuss the opening team time trial in Barcelona, and consider what Stage 1 could mean for the general classification battle. How much can teams gain or lose in the opening TTT? Who does the route suit? And what is the mood like inside a team bus just hours before the Tour gets underway? It’s part race preview, part reunion, and part all-access look at life inside the Tour de France. And this is just the start — For The Love Of Cycling will bring you daily episodes from the road throughout the Tour, with Ned and the team following the race across France. item.title: 431: The Big Tour de France Preview
item.description: There are now only two days until the Tour starts in Barcelona. All the teams have named their eight riders, so it’s time to look at the Danes, the favorites, the sprinters, and in general go through all 23 squads in this year’s Tour de France. Which team is stronger, UAE or Visma? Does Vingegaard have a chance against Pogacar? Who will win the team time trial? Can Remco and Lipowitz figure out how to share the captain role? Can Paul Seixas make it all the way and live up to the big French expectations? And who wins a Veloropa mug this week? It’s time for the big Tour de France preview. item.title: S14 Ep75: The XL Tour de France 2026 Preview | Part Three | Seixas, Remco, the Sprinters, and More
item.description: Join Lionel Birnie and Graham Willgoss as they complete a trilogy of episodes looking ahead to the 2026 Tour de France. With the big battle between Pogačar and Vingegaard covered in part one, and the route dissected in part two, this episode takes a deeper look at some of the other storylines and riders set to shape the race. From the much-anticipated debut of French sensation Paul Seixas, to the joint leaders of the Red Bull-Bora-Hansgrohe team, Remco Evenepoel and Florian Lipowitz, who else will challenge for the podium and the other top GC places? We assess a deep field of fast finishers and ask who will still be standing when the sprinting game of musical chairs comes to an end. There’s a visit to Coorevits Corner, we hear from the Voice of Radio Tour, and we ask which teams really need a strong Tour de France. Parts one and two of The XL Preview series are online now. Daily coverage of the Tour de France starts on Saturday, July 4. TITLE SPONSOR – MyWhoosh Our Tour de France preview series is sponsored by MyWhoosh. item.title: Damiano Caruso on Retirement, the Giro Miracle, and Pogačar vs. Vingegaard
item.description: Damiano Caruso is riding his eighth and final Tour de France. Before the start, he joins us to talk about Paul Seixas’ reported €13 million contract, why he thinks it puts unfair pressure on a 19-year-old, and why he would always choose a team that feels like family over a bigger paycheck. We also cover Bahrain Victorious’ chaotic run to the pink jersey at this year’s Giro d'Italia, his prediction for Pogačar vs. Vingegaard, and why, as he heads into retirement and a new role as directeur sportif, all he wants is to arrive in Paris without any regrets.

In this episode, Lionel Birnie and Graham Willgoss take an in-depth look at the route, picking out some highlights along the way. There’s also some French flavour from François Thomazeau, who has covered the Tour for the past four decades and writes the race’s official tourist guide. Join us for the journey when our daily episodes get underway after the opening team time trial on Saturday. Part one of The XL Preview series is online now. item.title: S14 Ep73: The XL Tour de France 2026 Preview | Part One | The Heavyweights
item.description: Will it be Tadej Pogačar or Jonas Vingegaard? There’s more to this year’s Tour de France than these two, of course, but it’s undeniable that the race for the maillot jaune will focus on the sport’s two heavyweights. The 2026 Tour de France starts here. There are just a few days to go until the Grand Départ in Barcelona, and The Cycling Podcast kicks off a three-part preview of the greatest race in the world by weighing up the two outstanding riders: champion Tadej Pogačar and challenger Jonas Vingegaard. Join Lionel Birnie and Graham Willgoss as they look at the two men who have dominated the Tour de France since 2020. Graham speaks to journalist Andy McGrath, author of Unstoppable, a biography of the Slovenian, about the man aiming to join the five-time winners’ club. And Lionel talks to a man our listeners will be very familiar with, Brian Nygaard, who has followed Vingegaard’s career from the beginning. Parts two and three of The XL Preview series will cover the route and all the other contenders to watch, and will be released soon. item.title: S1 Ep703: Is Cycling’s Craziest Tour de France Record About to Be Broken?
item.description: With the Tour de France returning to the legendary Alpe d’Huez this year, we investigate whether modern technology and superhuman form will finally see Marco Pantani’s “unbreakable” record fall. We also dive into a huge week for tech, featuring a custom Pharrell Williams x Louis Vuitton Pinarello and Canyon’s revolutionary new motor with an internal gearbox. Plus, we cover incredible endurance feats and debate the impact of “motodoping” in professional time trials.

item.title: The Most Stacked Field Ever? | Tour de France 2026 Preview
item.description: The biggest race in cycling is here. With just days to go until the 2026 Tour de France rolls out from Barcelona, Ned Boulting, David Millar, Lizzie Deignan and Pete Kennaugh break down everything you need to know ahead of this year’s race. Can Tadej Pogačar make history with a fifth Tour de France title and join the legendary ranks of Anquetil, Merckx, Hinault and Indurain? And if he does, is there anyone in the current peloton capable of stopping him? The team dives into this year’s route, from the opening team time trial in Barcelona to brutal mountain stages featuring Alpe d’Huez, the Galibier and the Tourmalet, and debates how the parcours could shape the battle for yellow. They also analyse the stacked start list: UAE’s powerhouse squad, Visma’s bold Giro-to-Tour strategy, Remco Evenepoel and Red Bull’s options, and Lidl-Trek’s hunt for stage wins. Plus, David, Lizzie and Pete share their own Tour de France experiences, from wearing yellow and riding as a domestique to what it really takes to survive the biggest race in the world. And this year, For The Love Of Cycling is on the road. Ned and David will be bringing you daily post-stage episodes from across France, recording from the race itself and bringing you closer to the Tour than ever before. Subscribe now and follow For The Love Of Cycling for daily Tour de France coverage throughout July. 🚙 Get 20% off your first carVertical report, or 50% off a full bundle, using the code FORTHELOVEOFCYCLING: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hQFMf_2pe8U For The Love Of Cycling is presented by Science in Sport. item.title: 430: Cort Wins the Danish Championships in His Farewell Season
item.description: Magnus Cort needed 11 attempts, but the last one was the charm, as he won the Danish road race championships in Herning the day after announcing his retirement, beating Anders Foldager in a tight sprint. Mads Pedersen and Lidl-Trek were the pre-race favourites, but as so often before, it proved difficult to carry the burden of that status. Despite many attacks and strong moves, Mads P had to settle for fifth place in the end, while Cort’s lead-out rider Anthon Charmig took bronze. Amalie Dideriksen won her sixth Danish road race title, while there were some sensational winners in both the women’s and men’s time trials: Sif Nikoline Bendix Madsen and Kristian Egholm. And 15-year-old Philip Hyldtoft from Tønder won the junior Danish championships, for riders under 19. Veloropa Podcast is presented by Bygma (win a Cervelo bike and a Jonas Vingegaard-signed jersey at bygma.dk/visma-partner), Betano (betano.dk) and Power (avis.power.dk).

item.title: The Big Tour de France Preview!
item.description: On Saturday, the 113th Tour de France rolls out of Barcelona: 21 stages, 3,333 kilometres to Paris, opening with a team time trial for the first time since 1971. For the sixth year running, it comes down to Tadej Pogačar against Jonas Vingegaard, and this time the Dane arrives off a crushing Giro win with his cleanest run-in since 2023. But two new faces are forcing their way into the picture: 19-year-old Paul Seixas, France's biggest home hope in decades, and a rebooted Remco Evenepoel at Red Bull. In this big preview episode, we go through all 21 stages one by one, from the Barcelona team time trial and the explosive Montjuïc finishes, through the early Pyrenean double of the Tourmalet and Gavarnie-Gèdre, the Bastille Day return to Le Lioran, the new summit finish at Plateau de Solaison and the Lake Geneva time trial, to the brutal Alpine double-header that ends on Alpe d'Huez twice. We talk through the green jersey rule changes and why they hand Tim Merlier a real shot, the Seixas pressure and that reported €13.5m contract talk, and Pogačar's chase for a record-equalling fifth title. We then work through the start lists team by team, UAE, Visma, Lidl-Trek, Decathlon and Red Bull, including the Affini fitness question, Lidl-Trek's crowded leadership, and Red Bull's two-leader puzzle with Evenepoel and Lipowitz. We finish with our predictions for the GC podium, the surprise names and breakaway riders to watch for three weeks, and our picks for yellow, green, polka dot and white. Tour de France 2026: Saturday 4 July to Sunday 26 July. Barcelona to Paris, 3,333 km, 54,450 m of climbing, 21 stages, rest days on 13 and 20 July. Five summit finishes, with Alpe d'Huez featuring twice in the final week. We're also launching Domestique Predict for the first time at this Tour. Pick one rider each day to win the stage, you can only pick each rider once, and we run it as a GC, so the rider's time counts, not their position. The overall winner takes home a Santini Tour de France yellow skinsuit. item.title: S2 Ep4: Seixas Has Been Offered the Biggest Contract in Cycling
item.description: In this week's GCN Racing News Show, we dive into the staggering reports that 19-year-old sensation Paul Seixas has been offered a record-breaking €13 million annual salary. We also analyse the growing list of absences in Jonas Vingegaard’s Tour de France support squad and what it means for his title defence. Finally, we wrap up a massive weekend of racing with a full round-up of National Championship results from across the globe.

item.title: Tour de France Preview and Danish Championships Recap
item.description: Forhjulslir is presented in collaboration with Continental Dæk Danmark. Continental is the most successful tire in the WorldTour peloton, from Tadej Pogačar in the Tour de France to Tobias Lund in the bunch sprints. Safety starts with the tire, both on the motorway and the bike path. The Danish flag belongs to Egholm and Cort, and the yellow bedding is in place. In this special edition of Forhjulslir, we look back at the weekend’s Danish Championships in Herning and ahead to the highlight of the summer: the Tour de France. Vingegaard versus Pogačar. Visma versus UAE. And a French teenager. We analyse the Danish Championships and warm up for the tactical and historic showdown that awaits in just a few days. The world’s biggest race, the biggest pressure, and the fight for the yellow jersey. The Tour starts on Saturday. We can’t wait! Featuring Emil Mielke Vinjebo and Anders Mielke. Per Bausager has gone into Tour exile before the Grand Départ, but he still gives his pick for the overall Tour podium in Paris at the end of the recording by phone.

item.title: Kristian Egholm Special
item.description: We have the newly crowned Danish time trial champion on the pod, or as our listeners know him, Lyd Preben. We start with Lyd Preben not being able to get the microphone to work, and end with Mads’ sound and picture disappearing. A proper Monday on a Monday. There is still a thorough review of the just-finished Danish Championships weekend in Herning. Enjoy, and remember that going forward we will record on Tour rest days and release as soon as possible afterward, or by Tuesday morning at the latest.

item.title: Paul Seixas’ Record-Breaking Contract Offer
item.description: In this episode, Patrick and Benji recap the news in the world of cycling. Exclusive deals from our trusted partners: Want to reach your goals with cycling’s smartest training app? item.title: #2 The Big Tour de France 2026 Preview
item.description: Episode 2 of 2 in this year’s big Tour preview. In this episode, you can hear about the general classification battle in this year’s Tour de France, the mountain-heavy final week in the world’s biggest bike race, and the experts’ picks for a Tour Manager team. We look at possible stage winners and the records on Alpe d’Huez. Guests: Christian Moberg Jørgensen and Mathias Mundbjerg. Support the show

item.title: S14 Ep72: Life Is Suisse
item.description: Rose Manley and Lizzy Banks discuss recent racing, with plenty of Tour de Suisse talking points and more. They look at how Zoe Backstedt might compare with Lorena Wiebes in future, what Kasia Niewiadoma’s rumoured move to Lidl-Trek could mean for both the Polish champion and Canyon-SRAM, and who Team UAE-ADQ should select for the Tour de France Femmes given their deep climbing roster. There is also a message for listeners from co-host Rebecca Charlton after she made her cancer treatment public last week. Plus: legless donkeys, Lizzy as the richest woman who never was, and how a poo could disrupt the Canyon-SRAM team camp.

item.title: Rebecca Charlton on Her Cancer Diagnosis, Cycling and Recovery
item.description: Matt Stephens speaks with one of his closest friends and colleagues, Rebecca Charlton, about the cancer diagnosis that led to the hardest year of her life. She approached her treatment the same way she approaches cycling: with full commitment and total dedication, the only way she knows how. Rebecca credits her cyclist mindset and her ability to endure suffering for helping her recovery, and she praises the cycling community for being able to turn up almost anywhere to talk about Matt Stephens’ balls. Support Mummy’s Star here: https://www.mummysstar.org/ Support Tour de Four here: https://www.tourde4.com/ www.sigmasports.com Learn more about your ad choices. Visit podcastchoices.com/adchoices

item.title: 429: A Full Tour Route Breakdown with Kasper Ankjærgaard
item.description: For many, this is the highlight of the year: our senior route correspondent Kasper Ankjærgaard, without a script or notes, breaks down this year’s Tour route. The Tour starts with a team time trial in Barcelona, the first time we have seen teams race a time trial on stage 1 of the Tour de France since 1971. Riders will climb Alpe d’Huez twice on two different stages. Montmartre is still included on some of the Paris laps, and the Tourmalet comes as early as the first week. Enjoy a wealth of detail about this year’s Tour route that you did not even know you wanted to hear. Veloropa Podcast is presented by Bygma (win a Cervelo bike and a signed Jonas Vingegaard jersey at bygma.dk/visma-partner), Betano (betano.dk) and Power (avis.power.dk).

item.title: #110 Gruppettoen on Forhjulslir: DM Week 🇩🇰
item.description: The heat is turned up and the air conditioning turned down at Hotel Østergaard in Herning, where Gruppettoen is gathered in room 216 on the evening before the Danish national time trial championships. Matson is dreaming in braces again, and tomorrow it all kicks off in the battle against the clock. Lidl-Trek are at DM week with a Tour-style setup: air conditioning, bus, truck, and new, very fast equipment. Get ready for DM week in Gruppettoen’s heatwave company. Featuring Anders Mielke and Mathias Sunekær Norsgaard. Gruppettoen on Forhjulslir is sponsored by Aioss, which has just launched a new juicy flavor, “flavored.” By buying Aioss, you are not only supporting us, but most importantly yourself, with more physical and mental energy in everyday life. item.title: 160. Ned Boulting: The Tour de France Special
item.description: This week, Robyn and Will are joined by Ned Boulting, who needs no introduction, but here’s one anyway: Ned broadcasts, commentates, pundits, speaks, performs, and writes about all things cycling. This year, Ned is away from the ITV commentary box at the Tour de France for the first time, and instead he’s taking his passion for two wheels on the road with his podcast For The Love Of Cycling. This summer, he’s joined by friends and former pros David Millar, Pete Kennaugh, and Lizzie Deignan as he follows the Tour around France, analysing the stages and telling the stories of the greatest cycling race on earth. In this episode, Ned talks us through the most important and potentially pivotal stages of this year’s Tour, the riders to watch, and the inevitable battle for third place, while Robyn introduces Ned to her self-styled “Vibes Classification,” which is surely destined to get a UCI points system and jersey of its own. item.title: #1 The Big Preview of Tour de France 2026
item.description: Episode 1 of 2 in this year’s big Tour preview. A huge showdown is shaping up as Jonas Vingegaard takes on Tadej Pogacar, young Paul Seixas, Remco Evenepoel, and many others in the French mountains during this year’s Tour de France. You can hear much more about that in this first of two episodes on this year’s Tour de France. We also talk about Mads Pedersen, Mattias Skjelmose, the Uno-X Danes, and a very special Grand Départ in Barcelona. Guests: Christian Moberg Jørgensen and Mathias Mundbjerg. Support the show.

item.title: What a Race! | Milano-Sanremo | Watts Occurring
item.description: G and Luke are joined by two-time Milano-Sanremo podium finisher and pod favourite Swifty to talk all things MSR. Watts Occurring is brought to you by ROUVY. Want a free month on ROUVY, on us? Use code WATTS when you sign up. Listen to What a Race! | Milano-Sanremo | Watts Occurring by Watts Occurring instantly on your tablet, phone, or browser — no downloads needed.

item.title: S14 Ep71: Pogačar Gets On a Swiss Roll
item.description: With the Tour de France now just ten days away, the Tour de Suisse was one of the final chances for riders to impress team management and earn selection for La Grande Boucle. Join Lionel Birnie and Graham Willgoss as they continue their countdown to the 113th edition of the greatest race in the world. After Jonas Vingegaard impressed at the Giro d’Italia, it was Tadej Pogačar’s turn to show his condition as he aims for a record-equalling fifth Tour title. In a shorter, but no less intense, edition of the Swiss stage race, Pogačar left little doubt that he is the number one favourite for the Tour. Who else impressed in Switzerland, Slovenia, and Belgium as the Tour approached? And which big-name star will miss the race, and what impact will that have on his team? We go deep into all of that in our final episode of The Cycling Podcast’s Attitude Camp. Next week, we complete our final preparations for the Tour de France with a bumper three-part preview, starting on Tuesday. item.title: S1 Ep702: Is the World’s Most Cycling-Friendly Country Getting Hostile?
item.description: This week on the GCN Show, we dive into the controversial trials of speed limits on bike paths in the Netherlands and ask whether they are actually enforceable. We also take a look at Canyon’s futuristic new Predict technology, featuring smart helmets and sensors that let your bike communicate with cars and traffic lights. Plus, we have the latest on Lael Wilcox’s record attempt, Sarah Ruggins’ monumental ride, and a breakdown of why Mathieu van der Poel was fined for his podium attire.

item.title: Has Pogačar Already Won the Tour?
item.description: Three-stage races, one clear message: the Tour de France is coming, and the favourites are flying. Tadej Pogačar turned his Tour de Suisse debut into a public training camp — a 69 km solo on stage 1, a time trial win by four-hundredths of a second over Mathieu van der Poel, and a mountain-stage win to finish. Three stages, the overall title, and no rival within six minutes. We break down the stage 1 glitch, Narváez’s 90 km breakaway raid in a thunderstorm, Van der Poel’s surprising time-trial form, and what Primož Roglič’s quiet week says about the twilight of his career. In Slovenia, Red Bull ran the show — Florian Lipowitz took the overall and two stages, Laurence Pithie won two more, in a near-perfect rehearsal 12 days before Lipowitz chases another Tour podium. And in Belgium, the Tour’s sprinters sharpened their knives: four bunch sprints, four different winners (Girmay, Merlier, Kooij, and Philipsen), with Philipsen climbing well, taking the overall, and looking like the early green-jersey favourite under the new points rules. Then the big one: Wout van Aert is out of the Tour, and the morning’s press points to a surprise replacement at Visma. We react to the Piganzoli reports, why Bram still wishes it were Ben Tulett, the back-to-back Grand Tour burnout question, and what it all means for Vingegaard’s bid to chase down Pogačar. item.title: Pogačar Unstoppable, Reusser Reigns Again
item.description: Tadej Pogačar once again proved why he’s the man to beat, dominating the Tour de Suisse with three stage victories and the general classification. Ned Boulting, David Millar, Lizzie Deignan, and Pete Kennaugh break down every key moment from the men’s race, from Pogačar’s astonishing long-range attacks and time trial victory to the performances of Richard Carapaz, Mathias Vacek, Mathieu van der Poel, and the breakaway winners who briefly stole the spotlight. The team also turns its attention to a thrilling and far less predictable women’s Tour de Suisse. Marlen Reusser secured the race for the third time in her career after a dominant time trial and final-stage performance, while Elisa Longo Borghini, Kasia Niewiadoma-Phinney, Cédrine Kerbaol, and rising star Lauren Dickson all played major roles in an action-packed week of racing. Plus, the panel discusses dramatic crashes, wrong turns, breakthrough rides, and the biggest stories from Switzerland as the road cycling season builds toward its biggest races. For The Love Of Cycling is presented by Science in Sport. This episode of For The Love Of Cycling is sponsored by Bikmo cycle insurance. Protect your ride before it’s too late. Learn more about your ad choices. Visit podcastchoices.com/adchoices.

item.title: The Best Pogačar Yet? Tour de Suisse Review
item.description: Johan Bruyneel and Spencer Martin break down Tadej Pogačar’s dominant overall victory at the Tour de Suisse and what his back-to-back stage wins at the end of the race say about his form, and whether we’re seeing the best version of Pogačar. They also discuss the rest of the field, including Mathieu van der Poel’s incredible time trial, Mathias Vacek’s breakout GC ride, and a quick look at Quinn Simmons’ win at the US National Championships. item.title: 428: DM preview and is Pogačar Tour-ready?
item.description: Tadej Pogačar swept aside all opposition in the Tour de Suisse as expected, but what impression did the Tour favorite leave when it comes to the numbers more and more people use to compare the top riders, even when they never race each other directly? Back home, most cycling fans are looking forward to the weekend, when the Danish National Championships take place in Herning. We look at the time trial, the road race, the favorites, and the outsiders. Of course, we’ll also do a quiz, and we’ll cover a few other news items from the cycling world, plus a brand-new documentary about Jonas Vingegaard. Veloropa Podcast is presented in collaboration with the streaming service Telmore Play (telmore.dk/veloropa), the betting provider Betano (betano.dk), and the electronics chain Power (avis.power.dk).

item.title: S2 Ep3: Pogi Says He’s Better Than Ever, and We Have No Reason to Doubt Him
item.description: In this week’s GCN Racing News, we analyze Tadej Pogačar’s incredible performance at the Tour de Suisse and what his “better than ever” form means for the upcoming Tour de France. We also discuss the major blow for Team Visma | Lease a Bike as Wout van Aert is confirmed to miss the Tour due to injury. Plus, we round up a packed week of racing, including the Women’s Tour de Suisse, the Tour of Belgium, and Tom Pidcock’s victory in Andorra.

item.title: Orange, Pink, and Yellow: The Luke Tuckwell Story
item.description: He grew up in Orange, Australia. Last year he wore the Maglia Rosa at the Baby Giro. A year later, he found himself in the yellow jersey at the Dauphiné, now known as the Tour Auvergne-Rhône-Alpes. This is the story of Luke Tuckwell, the 21-year-old Australian from Red Bull–BORA–hansgrohe who introduced himself to the cycling world with a breakthrough performance at this year’s Dauphiné. In this episode, Luke tells the story of his journey from Down Under to the top of European cycling. From riding in Orange and moving alone to Europe as a teenager, to wearing pink in Italy and yellow in France. He talks about the people who shaped his path, including Aussie figures like Simon Clarke, Mark Renshaw, Jai Hindley, and Jarrad Drizners, and about the life-changing grief of losing his younger sister last year after illness. This is not only a story about pink and yellow jerseys. It is a conversation with a remarkably reflective and mature young rider with huge dreams and even bigger potential. Hosted by Anders Mielke with special guest Luke Tuckwell. You can also watch the full interview on Half Wheeling’s YouTube channel: https://www.youtube.com/@HalfWheelingPodcast Watch the Tour de France from July 4–26 on Eurosport & TNT Sports, or stream it live on HBO Max.

item.title: Live from the Autobahn
item.description: On a shaky connection, we’re live from the Autobahn from Mads’ center console, right where his backyard mechanic’s gas can normally sits. Could we ask for more? We talk backyard mechanic racing, TOUR MERCH (26/6 at 12), the Danish National Championships in the center of the world, Herning, what 95 liters of 102-octane fuel actually costs, why remoulade is not allowed in carry-on luggage, how much Mads has ridden in his time trial, and why German gas stations are so ridiculous. That and much more in this episode of Lang Distance.

item.title: If Pogačar Is This Good, Can UAE Go 1-2 at the Tour?
item.description: Johan Bruyneel and Spencer Martin break down the first few stages of the Tour de Suisse, reflecting on Tadej Pogačar’s supreme dominance and what his ability to take time whenever he wants, even on non-bunch stages, says about the looming battle at the Tour de France. They also wonder whether the extremely high level of his UAE teammates and their ability to bully rivals at will could mean a UAE 1-2 at the Tour, with Jonas Vingegaard left fighting for third. item.title: GP5000 vs. Durano Plus and Everything You Need to Know About Important Cycling Tires
item.description: Cycling tires are more important than you might think. Things like low rolling resistance and weight, puncture resistance, durability, grip, and comfort all have to be packed into a tire that’s only a few millimeters thick and ideally easy to mount on the rim. In this episode, you get concrete buying recommendations, special things to watch for, and our own head-to-head test of “the people’s tires”: Continental Grand Prix 5000 vs. Schwalbe Durano Plus. Support the show.

item.title: 427: UAE dominance. Visma crisis. And Tobias second in the CPH sprint.
item.description: Claus and Stefan are on their own in this episode of Veloropa Podcast, as they’ve sent Kim to Norway on a survival trip. It came down to the wire for Danish sprinter Tobias Lund Andresen, who was beaten on the line in the second edition of the Danish WorldTour race Copenhagen Sprint. Abroad, Mexican rider Isaac Del Toro showed Tour de France form when he won the Tour Auvergne-Rhône-Alpes in dominant fashion, while his Tour captain Tadej Pogačar took everyone by surprise on the opening stage of the Tour de Suisse. Jonas Vingegaard and Visma | Lease a Bike, meanwhile, suffered a setback before the Tour, as superstar Wout van Aert has had to withdraw. There is also an update on the Tour of Belgium, which is unfortunately without Mads Pedersen, who was on the start list. Veloropa Podcast is presented by the Danish supplement Aioss (get up to 300 kroner off at aioss.dk), the streaming service Telmore Play (save up to 542 kroner at telmore.dk/veloropa), the betting provider Betano (see our betting tips at betano.dk), and the electronics chain Power (see the many great offers at avis.power.dk).

item.title: LeBlanq Joyride Festival Special
item.description: Matt Stephens takes us to the LeBlanq Joyride Festival on the Isle of Wight. You can expect conversations with Olympian Denise Lewis, Tour de France stage winner Sean Yates, and DJs Lisa Medwell and Taylor Phinney! www.sigmasports.com Learn more about your ad choices. item.title: S14 Ep70: Raging Bull
item.description: With the Tour de France just three weeks away, the mountain stages at the Tour Auvergne-Rhône-Alpes, aka the Dauphiné, were set to be a major test for two young riders who are due to make their debut in La Grande Boucle. Lionel Birnie and Graham Willgoss wrap up an enthralling week of racing in the French Alps as Mexico’s young star Isaac Del Toro continued his rise into one of the world’s finest stage racers. We know he’ll be a huge help to his UAE Team Emirates leaders Tadej Pogačar at the Tour, but could he be podium material in his own right? Meanwhile, French sensation Paul Seixas and his Decathlon team endured a setback — perhaps a timely reality check to temper the already sky-high expectations ahead of the Tour? item.title: S1 Ep701: Remco Won’t Win the Tour de France, But His Numbers Should
item.description: This week on the GCN Show, we look at Remco Evenepoel’s remarkable 425-watt FTP and discuss why durability may be the missing ingredient he needs to beat Pogačar and Vingegaard in Grand Tours. We also catch up on Lael Wilcox’s round-the-world record attempt, talk about Zwift’s ambitious new junior talent program, and take a look at Chris Froome’s new aqua bike career. Timestamps: 00:00 Welcome to the GCN Show; 02:00 Remco Evenepoel’s 425W FTP; 06:14 Can you train fatigue resistance?; 16:56 Cycling Shorts and Zwift’s Junior Team; 18:00 Lael Wilcox and ultra-endurance updates; 20:20 Chris Froome’s aqua bike race; 21:30 Tech news and the Empire State Trail; 25:10 Hacks and bodges; 30:08 Caption competition; 40:24 End-of-show nugget: Surströmming forfeit. Useful links: Join this channel for perks, subscribe so you don’t miss anything, explore GCN Insurance in the USA and UK, sign up for the GCN newsletter, use the GCN uploader, visit the GCN Shop, follow the GCN WhatsApp and Instagram broadcast channels, and join the GCN Club on Zwift to ride with us. Do you think Remco Evenepoel can improve his durability and fatigue resistance enough to beat Pogačar and Vingegaard at the Tour de France, or is it mostly genetics? Watch more on GCN via the Editor’s Choice and Features playlists. item.title: Answers from Tour Auvergne-Rhône-Alpes
item.description: Forhjulslir is presented in collaboration with Continental Tyres Denmark. Continental is the most successful tyre brand in the WorldTour peloton, from Tadej Pogačar in the rainbow jersey to Isaac del Toro in Auvergne and Tobias Lund in the bunch sprints. Safety starts with the tyre, both on the motorway and on the cycle path. We review last week’s Tour Auvergne-Rhône-Alpes with Del Toro’s samba-style pedalling, Seixas’ heroic riding and Decathlon’s foot fault, Visma’s pre-Tour concerns, Netcompany INEOS’ nightmare build-up, Lidl-Trek’s collective strength, Charmig’s beautiful victory, and much, much more. What answers did we get from Auvergne-Rhône-Alpes, formerly known as the Dauphiné, and who is left with more questions ahead of the Tour start in 15 days? Featuring Per Bausager and Anders Mielke.

item.title: Del Toro Is Locked In for P3 in Paris
item.description: The Critérium du Dauphiné is over, and a 22-year-old has announced himself to the world. Bram and Ethan break down Isaac Del Toro’s back-to-back summit wins on the Grand Colombier and Plateau de Solaison, Paul Seixas’ brave ride and painful abandon on the final morning, and what it all means for the Tour de France. Is Del Toro already a better climber than Remco Evenepoel? We think so. We also dig into the soaking-wet Copenhagen Sprint, which ended in carnage and gave Jasper Philipsen a major confidence boost before the Tour, and ask whether he can take the green jersey in 2026. Then it’s countdown time. Tadej Pogačar finally makes his Tour de Suisse debut, with Roglič and Carapaz also on the start line, and Red Bull brings a Grand Tour-worthy squad to the Tour of Slovenia. Plus: what on earth is going on with Matthew Brennan and Arnaud De Lie’s seasons? Fuel your body with something it actually recognises. No artificial anything. item.title: Vingegaard, Giro Safety, and Cycling’s Salary Problem with Michael Storer
item.description: Michael Storer has just delivered the best Grand Tour result of his career, finishing seventh overall at the Giro d’Italia for Tudor Pro Cycling. In this conversation, the Australian reflects on the race, explains why Jonas Vingegaard could beat Tadej Pogačar at the Tour de France, and offers a different perspective on cycling’s safety debate. We start with the Giro. Storer discusses the breakaway that reshaped the general classification, the crash that ended Adam Yates’ challenge, his strongest WorldTour time trial performance yet, and why sitting at the back of the peloton can sometimes be the safest tactical choice. From there, we go deep into rider safety. Storer questions the UCI’s proposed restrictions on cycling computer sizes, calls for greater transparency around the research behind new regulations, and explains why better roads, street sweepers, clearer warnings, and removing dangerous road furniture could have a much bigger impact. Then comes an unexpected idea. Rather than limiting the salaries of cycling’s biggest stars, Storer believes the sport should raise its minimum salary. He explains how low pay and short contracts can encourage riders to take greater risks, why two-year contracts could offer more security, and how reducing financial pressure may ultimately make racing safer. Storer also takes us inside life at a Grand Tour, from time trial recon and long hours on the team bus to reading Pride and Prejudice, watching Gossip Girl, and managing the mental fatigue of potentially racing the Giro and Tour de France back to back. Finally, his Tour prediction: based on what he saw firsthand at the Giro, Storer is backing Jonas Vingegaard to beat Tadej Pogačar this July.

item.title: The Next Tadej Pogačar?
item.description: All eyes were on one rider going into the 2026 Dauphiné, or Tour Auvergne-Rhône-Alpes 2026. By the time it was over, the actual winner had us wondering why we hadn’t always known he would win. Ned Boulting, David Millar, and Pete Kennaugh break down every stage of what turned out to be one of the most revealing weeks of racing before the Tour de France. Pete was on the ground as a DS and gives us the inside scoop on what it was like from the best seat in the house. The race may have left us with more questions than answers ahead of the Tour de France in July: Is Paul Seixas ready for the pressure of being France’s next Tour winner? Is Isaac Del Toro a GC contender for the Tour himself, or is he there purely in service of Tadej Pogačar? And why is Pete in a river? Oh, and it’s Pete’s birthday! For The Love Of Cycling is presented by Science in Sport. This episode is sponsored by Bikmo cycle insurance. Protect your ride before it’s too late. item.title: Is Isaac del Toro a Serious Tour de France Contender Now? | Dauphiné Review
item.description: Johan Bruyneel and Spencer Martin break down Isaac del Toro’s impressive win at the Tour Auvergne-Rhône-Alpes, also known as the Critérium du Dauphiné, and discuss what Del Toro’s dominant victory, Paul Seixas crashing out, Juan Ayuso looking strong in third, and the previously unknown rider Luke Tuckwell finishing second mean for the upcoming Tour de France. They dig into the tactics and moves that won Del Toro the race, debate whether this form means he can challenge for the podium at the Tour, and briefly preview this week’s Tour de Suisse. item.title: We Have Become Lords
item.description: Thanks to Frederik “sko” for giving us 1 square meter each, so we can now be addressed as Lord Pedersen and Lord Egholm. So much love in the inbox for Skjel, and we tip our hats. Our listeners are asking for pork all the way to the last merch drop, and Mads wants an Amager plate with 320. The C3 has passed away, and that is probably for the best. RIP. And you always have a Gråsten Vestegnspesto in the cup holder of your RS6 — that’s just how it is. That and much more in this episode of Lang Distance.

item.title: Can Seixas Come Back, and Is Pogačar Worried About Remco's Power Numbers?
item.description: Johan Bruyneel and Spencer Martin break down the first six stages of the Tour Auvergne - Rhône-Alpes, also known as the Critérium du Dauphiné, and discuss how the upside-down GC situation will play out heading into the final two brutal mountain stages. They also go through Remco Evenepoel’s recent release of power numbers and debate whether his unusual build-up to the Tour de France will pay off or lead to more frustration in stage racing. item.title: 426: Tobias Against the Stars and Victory for Prince Charmig
item.description: In this episode of the Veloropa Podcast, you can hear a longer interview with Tobias Lund Andresen, who on Sunday will take on almost the entire world’s sprint elite in the Danish WorldTour race Copenhagen Sprint. We look ahead to the race, examine the favorites and the route, and assess the Danish chances of success. We also discuss it with another Tobias, Tobias Hoff, who is the top person in charge of the entire event. Of course, we also stop by the Tour Auvergne-Rhone-Alpes, which is currently being raced, where we got a beautiful stage win for Anthon Charmig during the week. And Tadej Pogacar will be back in action next week, when the other major Tour de France warm-up race, the Tour de Suisse, takes place. item.title: 159. Lidl-Trek's Mattias Reck: Why Are Riders Faster, and the Resilience of Mads Pedersen
item.description: This week, Will and James are joined by Lidl-Trek head coach Mattias Reck. Born and raised in Sweden, Mattias started out racing as a pro before moving into performance coaching, like many people in his profession. In 2017, he joined Trek-Segafredo, where he soon found himself developing the talent of a young Mads Pedersen. Swedish and Danish are pretty similar, says Mattias, which certainly helped with coaching. Today that relationship continues — Mads is almost like family now — and Mattias is also personally responsible for a range of other riders, mostly the Danish ones, plus women’s WorldTour stars Shirin van Anrooij and Fleur Moors. We spoke with Mattias just after Paris-Roubaix, where Pedersen finished seventh. It was not quite the result the team had hoped for at the start of the Classics campaign, but it was still a victory of sorts given Pedersen’s awful crash at the Volta a la Comunitat Valenciana in early February, which required surgery and kept him out of racing until late March. Here, Mattias explains the emotional highs and lows for rider and coach when things go wrong; the mental strength needed for rehab; and the care teams provide, from check-ins to conversations with team psychologists to the role of e-cycling platforms such as Rouvy in recovery. Mattias also explains why he thinks riders are getting faster, the latest thinking in nutrition and carbohydrate fueling, the importance of fatigue resistance, the shift in training philosophy, and why cycling needs to be careful not to exploit young talent or burn out careers too early. Chat begins at 9:50 ----------------- This episode is brought to you by the Hammerhead Karoo GPS bike computer. item.title: S14 Ep69: Team Time Trial and Error
item.description: Who would have thought it possible to take such a deep dive into a team time trial stage? Join Lionel Birnie and Graham Willgoss as they recap the story of the Tour Auvergne-Rhône-Alpes — the race formerly known as the Critérium du Dauphiné — so far. After two entertaining breakaway wins to open the race, the new team time trial format finally captured attention, perhaps because it serves as a dress rehearsal for the Tour de France opener in Barcelona in a few weeks. How did the teams compare? Which squads got it right, and who still has work to do? And now that they have reached the halfway point, who looks best placed to take the yellow jersey in the mountains? item.title: Is Demi Vollering One of the All-Time Greats?
item.description: Where does Demi Vollering now stand in the conversation about the greatest riders of all time? Ned Boulting and Lizzie Deignan sit down for a full race breakdown, covering nine days of racing that swung wildly between controversy, comeback, and history being made. Nine stages. A disqualification nobody saw coming. A comeback time trial that raised eyebrows throughout the peloton. And one rider who left Italy having rewritten the history books. item.title: Seixas Should Win the Dauphiné, but How He Wins Is Everything
item.description: The Critérium du Dauphiné 2026, now the Tour Auvergne-Rhône-Alpes, is wide open, and 19-year-old Paul Seixas arrives as the favourite ahead of Isaac del Toro. Two days in, the breakaway has won both stages through Alex Baudin and Anthon Charmig, while the GC riders wait. We break down stages 1 and 2, why João Almeida is out of the Tour de France, the team time trial, and the brutal mountain finale on the Grand Colombier and Plateau de Solaison. Plus: Demi Vollering's stunning comeback to win the Giro d'Italia Women 2026, becoming only the second rider ever to complete all three women's Grand Tours. Fuel your body actually recognises. item.title: Mads P moves to Nakskov
item.description: NOT... But I’m sure he’d love it down there. Late on a Sunday evening, we just manage to get another episode in the can. There’s a bit about narrow asses and wide saddles — or is it the other way around? We can never remember what we’ve talked about, and that goes for this text too, which I (Jakob) am writing. The conversation is as unfiltered and honest as always. And yes, Mads has this week’s home. That and much more in this episode of Lang Distance.

item.title: S14 Ep68: Stage 9 | Saluzzo - Saluzzo
item.description: Could this really be the end? Join Rebecca Charlton and Lizzy Banks for one final installment from the Giro d’Italia Women as the winner of the maglia rosa is finally decided on an explosive day. We also hear from key names in the GC battle, Lidl-Trek’s Isabella Holmgren, and Canyon SRAM’s Antonia Niedermaier, who was virtual leader on the road earlier in the stage. And don’t forget to catch up on our full race coverage as The Cycling Podcast Féminin brings you daily coverage from the race. Also on the agenda: heart over legs, wonky helmet straps, and, of course, cheese. item.title: Denmark’s wildest amateur rider: From a 220-kilo bench press to insane numbers on the bike
item.description: In ten years, Patrick Kinch has gone from bench pressing 220 kilos to riding 40,000 kilometers a year and posting one of the world’s best times on Alpe du Zwift in 2026. His body weight has also dropped from 120 to 71 kilos. These are such extreme achievements that they are hard to comprehend. Meet Patrick Kinch in this episode, where his performance is also assessed by Thomas Vestergaard Bundgaard, cycling coach and bike fitter. Support the show

item.title: S14 Ep66: Stage 8 | Rivoli to Sestriere
item.description: Bow down before your queen! Yes indeed, it’s time for the queen stage at the Giro d’Italia Women on a route that was meant to include the Colle delle Finestre and finish in Sestriere, but best-laid plans turned icy and chaos followed. Join Rebecca Charlton and Lizzy Banks as they take a closer look at all the events of Stage 8, where there was drama, a sheet of ice, and a makeshift finish line. We also hear from Dani Rowe MBE from the top of a mountain, and Marlen Reusser drops in for a chat. Don’t forget to listen all week as The Cycling Podcast Féminin brings you daily coverage from the race. We hear all the “what if?” theories ahead of the final stage, and on the agenda is also our angel of cheese, Denny Gray. Will he have strayed from the mainstream? Tune in to find out. item.title: 425: Skjelmose and Seixas test their Tour form
item.description: The Giro is over, with Jonas Vingegaard setting a Danish record with his victory. Now attention turns to the build-up to the year’s next major goal: the Tour de France. Vingegaard will rest and head to training camp, while other big Tour names will test themselves against each other in what we used to know as the Dauphiné Libéré and Criterium du Dauphiné, and which is now called the Tour Auvergne-Rhône-Alpes. The favorite is only 19 years old. He is French, and his name is Paul Seixas, the new hope of a French Tour victory, which they have not had since Bernard Hinault in 1985. Seixas is the natural favorite after a strong spring, while the biggest Danish name in the race is Mattias Skjelmose, who in the Tour is expected to work as a luxury helper for new signing Juan Ayuso at Lidl-Trek. We look at the route, the favorites, and the actually rather few Danes who have so far been selected for the Auvergne-Rhône-Alpes race, which starts on Sunday. Veloropa Podcast is presented by our new partner, Telmore Play (telmore.dk/veloropa), as well as betting provider Betano (betano.dk) and electronics chain Power (avis.power.dk).

item.title: S14 Ep64: Not the Dauphiné
item.description: The build-up to the 2026 Tour de France starts here. It’s not the Dauphiné, because the race has been rebranded as the Tour Auvergne-Rhône-Alpes, which may take some getting used to. Join Lionel Birnie and Graham Willgoss as they reunite to look ahead to the first major preparation race for the Tour, which starts on Sunday. With Jonas Vingegaard resting after a convincing victory at the Giro d’Italia, and Tadej Pogačar focusing on the Tour de Suisse, the spotlight falls on the young French sensation Paul Seixas. The Decathlon rider won Itzulia earlier in the spring, adding to the growing hype around him. France is starting to believe it may finally have a successor to Bernard Hinault, the last French rider to win the Tour. But who stands in his way next week, and what other clues will we get about who is on track for the Tour? item.title: Budget caps, safety, and the future of GreenEdge ft. Brent Copeland
item.description: Brent Copeland rarely gives interviews. As General Manager of GreenEdge Cycling (Team Jayco AlUla and Liv AlUla Jayco) and President of the AIGCP, the teams’ union, he sits in almost every room where cycling’s future is being decided. In this conversation, he opens up about all of it. We start with the Giro d’Italia: the stage 2 crash that took out Andrea Vendrame and changed the entire race, and what the Tour de France build-up looks like with Michael Matthews in the mix. From there we go deep into SafeR, the body working on rider safety. Copeland explains how every crash in pro cycling is now logged and analysed, the real story behind the handlebar width rule, and what actually happened in the SRAM gear restriction case. Then the big issue: money. Copeland makes the case for a budget cap in professional cycling. He explains how average WorldTour budgets have nearly tripled since 2012, why losing Mauro Schmid came down to an offer 40% over market price, what he learned from the people who introduced salary caps to the AFL and NRL, and why he believes the current model is unsustainable. He also addresses the question every Australian fan is asking: what happens to the team when Gerry Ryan steps back?

item.title: Vingegaard’s Giro and the giants’ race
item.description: Forhjulslir is presented in collaboration with Continental Dæk Danmark. Continental is the most successful tire in the WorldTour peloton, from Tadej Pogačar in the rainbow jersey to Felix Gall and Filippo Ganna in the Giro. Safety starts with the tire, both on the highway and on the bike path. We put Jonas Vingegaard’s Giro victory to bed and discuss the biggest and most memorable moments, as well as which version of Jonas Vingegaard we have seen over the past three weeks. Has he come closer to Tadej Pogačar? Can you even answer that question without Pogačar being there? And of course we also need to talk about Uno-X’s first Giro, with Vinjebo behind the wheel as sports director. There is also movement in the transfer market. It came as a bombshell the day after the Giro that Grischa Niermann is leaving Team Visma | Lease a Bike to join Lidl-Trek, which continues to strengthen. What perspectives lie in the giants’ race, which is unfolding outside the peloton these weeks? Finally, we look at the mountainous Dauphiné (Tour Auvergne-Rhône-Alpes), which starts on Sunday, with Seixas, Del Toro, and Ayuso on the start list. Participants: Per Bausager, Emil Mielke Vinjebo, and Anders Mielke.

item.title: #31 Bak Lane: The Latest News with Michael Valgren
item.description: Join us as we look back at the Giro and Michael Valgren’s beautiful stage win on stage 17. “Valle” joins Bak Lane for a timely episode about Valgren’s Italian triumph, the thoughts and decisions in the Giro peloton, Vingegaard, Valle’s goals for the rest of the season, and much, much more. Host: Lars Bak. Edited by Anders Mielke (Forhjulslir).

item.description: Yes, it’s time for one rider after another to go up a hill, because it’s time trial day at the Giro d’Italia Women! We also hear from young French talent Solène Muller about her time in the race’s hot seat. And don’t forget to listen all week as The Cycling Podcast Féminin brings you daily coverage from the race. item.title: S1 Ep699: The Illegal Bike That Got a Rider Thrown Off the Giro
item.description: This week, Si and Dan dive into the controversy around the UCI’s strict 6.8 kg minimum bike weight rule after one rider was disqualified for being just 20 grams under it! They also wrap up the biggest tech talking points and wild tactics from Unbound Gravel, look at the rise of the huge 32-inch wheel standard (?!), and debate night-time cycling visibility. Plus, there’s a really exceptional Super Nugget for the 16 who make it there! 👀 ⏱️ Timestamps ⏱️ 00:00 - Welcome to the GCN Show! 01:01 - The 20g UCI Bike Weight Disqualification 15:06 - Cycling Shorts: Unbound Gravel Tech & Tactics 19:07 - Are 32-Inch Wheels The Future of Gravel & MTB? 20:58 - Race News 22:14 - Road Safety & New Cargo Bike Collab! 27:04 - Hack / Bodge 32:53 - Caption Competition 35:27 - Comments Of The Week 42:43 - Super Nugget!

item.title: #3 Giro Diaries
item.description: The Giro d’Italia 2026 is over. It was a historic edition, with Jonas Vingegaard becoming only the eighth cyclist to win all three Grand Tours. Don’t worry about your Giro blues... Join Anders Mielke as he looks back on the third and final week of the Giro d’Italia 2026 together with colleagues from Eurosport and voices from the peloton and the cycling family. item.title: What We Have Learned from the Giro
item.description: Jonas Vingegaard wins the 2026 Giro d’Italia with five stage victories and a final margin of more than five minutes, completing the Grand Tour treble. But was it the race we hoped for? Bram and Ethan break down everything from the Giro: the GC battle that was decided early, a surprise podium, the breakout of Alfonso Eulálio, Felix Gall’s second place as a message to his own team, Jai Hindley’s love affair with Italian racing, the sprint battle between Jonathan Milan and Paul Magnier, Toon Aerts’ unexpected emergence, and the wildly uneven team performances from Visma, Bahrain, Lidl-Trek, and Soudal Quick-Step. Plus: what the Giro tells us about the Tour de France — and whether anyone can actually challenge Vingegaard and Pogačar in July. This episode is fuelled by MNSTRY - 100% vegan sports nutrition trusted by Unibet Rose Rockets, CANYON//SRAM and more. item.title: Giro Rest Day Reflections 2026 Stages 16–21: Vingegaard Makes It Look Easy
item.description: Technically, it’s not a rest day again; the Giro d’Italia 2026 has just been won. As the dust settles in Rome, Matt Stephens reflects on the final week of racing from behind the scenes at The Breakaway studio for TNT Sports / HBO Max. Expect opinions, views, and perspectives from the final six stages, before Matt shares his thoughts on this year’s Giro as a whole and looks ahead to a mouthwatering Tour de France showdown between a dominant Jonas Vingegaard and perhaps the only man who can beat him: Tadej Pogačar. www.sigmasports.com/ Learn more about your ad choices. item.title: Skjelmose Special
item.description: A very special episode with Mattias Skjelmose. Skjel and Jakob paddle a bit at the start and are later joined by Mads. Yes, it was a late flight home from the race. Pure chaos to the end: can you get a twindog, when was the last time Jonas got a meat whistle, TTT training that went wild, and what wouldn’t Mads do for a klamphugger? An episode that goes in every direction, and heartfelt thanks for joining this episode of Lang Distance Skjel.

item.title: Vingegaard's Victory Lap, Milan's Last Chance, and Rome Signs Off | Giro d'Italia 2026 Stage 21 Podcast
item.description: Stage 21 of the Giro d'Italia 2026 sees the race roll into Rome with Jonas Vingegaard already the story of the three weeks, but the peloton still had one more day to race. Vingegaard crushed the general classification, becoming the first rider of his era to win all three Grand Tours. It was a victory lap, but cycling never really does those quietly, and Rome made sure of that. Jonathan Milan had been waiting for this moment. Stage after stage, the sprint finishes had slipped away. Today, at last, he got his chance. It was a thrilling battle at the front, and Milan took the win. This Giro produced characters that will stay with you: Eulalio, winning hearts from start to finish in the white jersey; Ciccone, racing with everything he had every day, heart on his sleeve; and Magnier, young and fearless, dominating the sprint stages before Milan had his say. Ned Boulting and Jacopo Guarnieri are here to close out the Giro d'Italia 2026 from the finish line. For The Love Of Cycling is presented by Science in Sport. This episode of For The Love Of Cycling is sponsored by Bikmo cycle insurance. Protect your ride before it’s too late. Learn more about your ad choices. Visit podcastchoices.com/adchoices

item.title: Giro d'Italia 2026 Review Live from the Wattage Festival with Bradley Wiggins
item.description: Live from the Wattage Festival in Oostende, Johan Bruyneel, Bradley Wiggins, and Spencer Martin break down Jonas Vingegaard's overall win at the Giro d'Italia, making him only the eighth rider in history to win all three Grand Tours. They discuss what, if anything, his rivals could have done to stop him and, of course, what this means for his looming battle with Tadej Pogačar at the Tour de France. They also briefly discuss Jonathan Milan's win in the Stage 21 sprint in Rome and what it means for him and his Lidl-Trek team. item.title: 424: Historic Vingegaard in the Giro
item.description: In this episode of Veloropa Podcast, we cover a host of Danish victories, but none as impressive as Jonas Vingegaard's overall win in the Giro d'Italia, which made him historic as the first Dane to win all three Grand Tours — and only the eighth rider in history! We place Vingegaard's achievement in both international cycling history and Danish sporting history, including a special edition of Plesner's Power Ranking of the greatest Danish individual sporting achievements of all time. Of course, we also look back at the Giro's final three stages: the queen stage that brought a surprise win for “600 meter Kuss,” Saturday's stage, which gave Jonas Vingegaard his fifth stage victory in this Giro d'Italia, and the relieving sprint win for home favorite Jonathan Milan in Rome. We have also selected a team of eight young riders from this year's Giro whom we consider the race's biggest revelations. Listen in and see whether you agree — and whether you can do as well as Plesner and Djurhuus in this episode's quiz. Veloropa Podcast is presented in collaboration with Bygma (win a Cervelo bike and a signed Visma jersey at bygma.dk/visma-partner), Betano (betano.dk), and Power (avis.power.dk).

item.title: Is This the Best Jonas Vingegaard We've Seen? | Giro d'Italia 2026 Stage 20
item.description: Stage 20 of the 2026 Giro d'Italia saw Jonas Vingegaard finish his race the only way he knows how: with a fifth stage win. That also puts his name in cycling’s smallest club: riders who have won all three Grand Tours. Giulio Ciccone wrapped up the mountains classification, Eulalio had done enough to secure the white jersey, and Kuss, emptied by yesterday’s effort, looked like a different rider today. That’s what three weeks of Grand Tour racing does to you. There had been talk of Visma giving the stage to Piganzoli, but after Visma had burned through its riders earlier than usual, Jonas decided to put the stage to bed. Behind, Bernal emptied the tank for his teammate Arensman to hold onto fourth place, losing the podium spot to Jai Hindley. Ned Boulting and Jacopo Guarnieri report from the finish line. For The Love Of Cycling is presented by Science in Sport. This episode of For The Love Of Cycling is sponsored by Bikmo cycle insurance. Protect your ride before it’s too late. item.title: Sepp Kuss Makes History in the Dolomites | Giro d'Italia 2026 Stage 19
item.description: Stage 19 of the 2026 Giro d’Italia took the race deep into the Dolomites, and Visma looked so in control that they were comfortable sending Sepp Kuss up the road in the breakaway. It was a clear sign of just how confident they are in the general classification battle. Elsewhere, it was a tougher day. Jhonatan Narváez and Ethan Vernon both abandoned the race, while EF Education-EasyPost made the difficult decision to withdraw Michael Valgren and James Shaw from the Giro. Ned Boulting and Jacopo Guarnieri report from the finish line. For The Love Of Cycling is presented by Science in Sport. This episode of For The Love Of Cycling is sponsored by Bikmo cycle insurance. item.title: The Perfect Leadout and the Best Sprinter | Giro d'Italia 2026 Stage 18 Podcast
item.description: Stage 18 of the 2026 Giro d'Italia and, for the sprinters, the window is almost closed. Polti VisitMalta attacked again from the break. Eulalio crashed and then lit up the final climb, but Visma stayed composed and brought the move back with ease. Then it came down to the sprint. Lidl-Trek were determined to deliver for Milan, but the positioning wasn't there. Stuyven and Magnier made it look straightforward, and before Milan could react, the door had closed. One sprint left: Rome. That's it. Ned Boulting and Jacopo Guarnieri bring all the reaction and analysis from Stage 18. For The Love Of Cycling is presented by Science in Sport. item.title: 423: A Beautiful Valgren Victory, Jonas Dominates
item.description: Michael Valgren's win on Stage 17 of this year's Giro d'Italia was beautiful, spectacular, and emotional. The rider from Thy, who four years ago feared for both his career and his life after a severe crash at the Route de Occitanie, finally got the Grand Tour victory that now adds to an already impressive résumé. And if Valgren's win wasn't enough for Danish cycling fans, Jonas Vingegaard strengthened his lead in the Giro with a secure fourth stage victory on Tuesday, while Julius Johansen secured victory in the prologue at Boucles de la Mayenne shortly after the episode ended, where Mads P will also make his comeback after his turbulent cobbled classics. As if that weren't enough, Mads Landbo and Malthe Risbjerg also won races today, Thursday. We look back at the week's three Giro d'Italia stages, of course focusing on the Danish performances, but also the somewhat surprising battle for the points jersey and today's surprising sprint in Pieve di Soligo. We also look ahead to the Giro's three decisive stages this weekend. Veloropa Podcast is presented in collaboration with Bygma (win a Cervelo bike and a signed Visma jersey at bygma.dk/visma-partner), Betano (betano.dk), and Power (avis.power.dk).

item.title: 158. Magdeleine Vallieres, World Champion
item.description: This week, Robyn is joined by road race world champion Magdeleine Vallieres. The 24-year-old EF Education-Oatly rider was not considered one of the main favorites when the women's peloton lined up at the start of last year's course in Rwanda, but after one of the hardest World Championships in years, she was a convincing winner, attacking clear from the lead group of three at the bottom of the final climb. In the process, she took Canada's first-ever rainbow jersey on the road, a year before the Worlds heads to Montreal, just two hours from Magdeleine's hometown. In this episode, Magdeleine talks about that day in Kigali — the buildup, the execution, the reaction, the celebration — and this year's Worlds in Canada, as well as becoming a leader, riding for GC, the evolution of women's Grand Tours, and falling in love with cycling on a 1,000 km bikepacking adventure at age nine. Chat begins at 3:08 ----------------- This episode is brought to you by the Hammerhead Karoo GPS bike computer. item.title: Attack After Attack! Breakaway Battle Explodes | Giro d'Italia 2026 Stage 17 Podcast
item.description: Stage 17 of the Giro d'Italia was packed with attacks. Ciccone tried once again to force the breakaway to stick, but it didn't work and his chances are running out. After the final move stayed clear, the stage seemed set for a reduced bunch sprint, until one rider refused to wait and attacked with 1 km to go. Michael Valgren claimed his first Grand Tour stage win on his 12th Grand Tour appearance, at 34 years old. EF Education-EasyPost finally got their first win of this Giro. Then there is the maglia ciclamino battle. The contest between Narváez and Magnier has been building quietly all race, and after today it is the most compelling storyline in this Giro. Ned Boulting and Jacopo Guarnieri report from the finish line. For The Love Of Cycling is presented by Science in Sport. This episode of For The Love Of Cycling is sponsored by Bikmo cycle insurance. item.title: Pace, Heat and an Emotional Comeback Win | Giro d'Italia 2026
item.description: Johan Bruyneel and Spencer Martin break down Michael Valgren's incredible win after a late solo attack from the breakaway on a hot, hard day through the foothills of the Dolomites. They discuss what the victory means for Valgren, who has come a long way back from a terrible crash in 2022, and how important it is for his EF team, which has struggled to make an impact so far in the race. They also preview tomorrow's Stage 18, explaining who they think will win and how it may unfold. item.title: Jonas Vingegaard Is Unstoppable! | Giro d'Italia 2026 Stage 16 Podcast
item.description: Stage 16 of the 2026 Giro d'Italia saw Jonas Vingegaard make it four summit finishes out of four. Bellinzona to Carì was only 113 kilometres, but it featured more than 3,000 metres of climbing. Visma were not giving up control today. The breakaway was kept on a tight leash, and when Decathlon took over with 35 km to go, the race was about to explode. Piganzoli ripped the field apart before Jonas even launched. Pellizzari was dropped early. Ciccone, frustrated after another missed chance in the break, sat up with 17 km to go. And when Jonas attacked with 6.6 km to go, nobody could follow. David Millar and Pete Kennaugh step in for Ned and Jacopo to unpack it all. Ned and Jacopo are back tomorrow for Stage 17 coverage as usual. For The Love Of Cycling is presented by Science in Sport. This episode of For The Love Of Cycling is sponsored by Bikmo cycle insurance. Protect your ride before it’s too late. Learn more about your ad choices. Visit podcastchoices.com/adchoices

item.title: FTP, TSS and IF: Training Metrics and Why You Might Not Need Them
item.description: When you've been out on a ride, both Strava and your bike computer spit out a lot of numbers. You may come across abbreviations like TSS and IF. Or what about NP or FTP? In this episode, you can hear about how to use these numbers in your training - and why you might want to throw them in the bin and take a much more practical approach instead. Guests are Thomas Vestergaard Bundgaard and Jonas Lindberg. Support the show

item.title: S1 Ep698: How Weak or Strong Are Cyclists?
item.description: In this week's GCN Show, we dive into the long-running debate about strength versus power and ask: are professional cyclists actually 'weak' in the traditional sense? We look at the latest pro power data from the Giro d'Italia and explore why more riders are now adding heavy gym work to their training. Plus, we cover the latest headlines, including SRAM's legal victory against the UCI over gear ratios, a 93-year-old taking on Unbound 200, and an auction for Albert Einstein's bike saddle! Chapters: ⏱️ 00:00 - Welcome to the GCN Show 01:31 - Pro Power Data from the Giro 02:27 - Are Pro Cyclists Weak? (Power vs. Strength) 04:50 - The Rise of the 'Gym Bro' Cyclist 07:58 - Our Final Verdict on Strength 09:19 - 93-Year-Old Fred Schmid's Unbound Attempt 11:46 - Tech News: Hunt Wheels & Einstein's Saddle 14:19 - Hack or Bodge of the Week 16:04 - Caption Competition & Comments of the Week

item.title: S14 Ep51: One-Hit Wonder
item.description: The 2016 Giro d’Italia was the first edition The Cycling Podcast covered from start to finish on the ground, and it turned out to be a memorable race. A dramatic final week saw Steven Kruijswijk fall at the last hurdle, almost literally, and Vincenzo Nibali mount an extraordinary comeback. But neither story could match stage 15 to Alpe di Siusi, and especially the shock victory of outsider Alexander Foliforov. Foliforov disappeared almost as quickly as he arrived, retiring two years after his Giro stage win. Since then he has become a meme, a kind of post-ironic legend, but above all an enigma: what mysterious force carried him to victory that day, and where has he gone over the last ten years? A decade on from the Russian’s greatest day, Daniel Friebe tracked him down and invited Foliforov to relive and explain one of the strangest days and results in recent Giro history. One-Hit Wonder was written and produced by Daniel Friebe. Music is by Amaraterra. item.title: How Uno-X Pulled Off the Perfect Breakaway Win with Johannes Kulset
item.description: Uno-X Mobility’s Johannes Kulset joins us fresh off a huge Giro stage win for the team. Fredrik Dversnes got into the break on a flat sprint stage and executed a plan the team had been sitting on since day one. Johannes explains exactly how it happened, why the win means more than most people realize, and what the team is chasing in the final week. We also discuss why the Giro and Vuelta are producing better racing than the Tour right now, what it actually feels like to get dropped by Vingegaard while he’s nose-breathing, the moto controversy and why Kulset thinks the peloton is mostly reading it wrong, and the inside story on Dversnes’ celebration, which nearly gave the whole team a heart attack.

item.title: 422: Jonas in Pink and a Norwegian Sensation
item.description: As expected, Jonas Vingegaard took the pink leader’s jersey on Saturday’s mountain stage to Pila after a strong team performance from Visma, with Tim Rex especially emptying himself. That ended the adventure for Portuguese rider Afonso Eulalio, who is still second overall. Sunday was supposed to be a sprinter’s feast in Milan, but a Norwegian outsider beat the big favorites on a stage marked by protests over the road surface, motorcycles, and even the expulsion of an Italian rider from the Giro. In this episode of the Veloropa Podcast, we look at Jonas Vingegaard’s current level, his teammates’ efforts, and the exciting stages ahead in the Giro d’Italia, where the points competition has gained extra spice thanks to breakaway king Jhonatan Narvaez. Veloropa Podcast is presented in collaboration with Bygma (win a Cervelo and a signed Visma jersey at bygma.dk/visma-partner), Betano (join Gæt n’ get on Betano’s Instagram and Facebook page), and the electronics chain Power (find current offers at avis.power.dk).

item.title: Giro d'Italia Women Preview with Elisa Longo Borghini
item.description: Ned and Lizzie Deignan sit down to talk about the Giro Donne: how far the race has come since the early days, what is still missing, and why this year’s edition feels like a turning point. They discuss the calendar change, the stages that will shape the GC battle, and the riders who could shake things up. Then Lizzie’s former teammate and rival Elisa Longo Borghini joins the conversation. They talk about what it is like to race the Giro on home roads, the weight of the maglia rosa when you are Italian, and how different the race looks now compared with when she first lined up. Elisa opens up about her bid to win it for a third straight year — the pressure, the motivation, and why she still races with the same fire she had on day one. For The Love Of Cycling is presented by Science in Sport. This episode of For The Love Of Cycling is sponsored by Bikmo cycle insurance. Protect your ride before it’s too late. item.title: S14 Ep50: Viva Somarriba!
item.description: Pour yourself a sparkling water and join Rose Manley, Denny Gray, and Rebecca Charlton as they review the Spanish stage-racing block. With many of the big names away preparing for the upcoming Giro d’Italia, the short stage races of Itzulia and Vuelta a Burgos delivered plenty of surprises and breakthrough performances. In a rare chance away from domestique duties, Mischa Bredewold led SD Worx, who looked back to their dominant best. But what does that win mean for a team that already has so many champions in its roster? Yara Kastellijn’s big victory on the final day of Burgos capped a remarkable few weeks for a rider who had not won since her breakthrough at the Tour de France Femmes in 2023. Plus, did FDJ United Suez make the wrong call in Itzulia by not backing young British star Lauren Dickson? In our mini feature, we speak to the last Spanish rider before Paula Blasi this year to win a women’s Grand Tour. That champion is Joane Somarriba, who won the Giro Femminile twice and the Grand Boucle, the Tour de France Femmes precursor, three times. She talks about her rise to the top, overcoming hospitalization and prejudice to become one of the best riders of the late 1990s and early 2000s. We also look ahead to this year’s Giro d’Italia Women, which begins at the end of the month. The Cycling Podcast Féminin will bring listeners daily coverage of the race for the first time, starting on 30 May. Also on the agenda: the atmosphere of shopping malls, virtual sangrias, and Denny’s dream of being paid in cheese.

That and much more in this episode of Lang Distance.

item.description: A day that was supposed to be made for the fast men instead saw the breakaway defy every expectation. Fredrik Dversnes, on his Grand Tour debut, crossed the line first while the peloton scrambled behind him. Jonathan Milan missed another opportunity. The sprint teams couldn’t organize, couldn’t cooperate, and couldn’t catch the riders up the road. So how many sprint days does Milan actually have left? item.title: Jonas Vingegaard’s Devastation on the Stage from Hell | Giro d'Italia 2026 Stage 14 Podcast
item.description: Stage 14 of the 2026 Giro d’Italia was the hardest day by far. Ciccone was in the break again, but it was always going to be a tough ask today. Visma rode at the front of the peloton for almost the entire stage, and on the final climb it played out exactly as everyone expected. Campenaerts, Kuss, and Piganzoli each took turns closing the gap to the break before Vingegaard let rip and proved everyone right. Gall rode his own race at his own pace. Eulálio lost the pink jersey, but not by as much as the mountain profile suggested. And Piganzoli? What a ride. Ned Boulting and Jacopo Guarnieri report from the finish line with all the reaction and analysis. For The Love Of Cycling is presented by Science in Sport. item.title: Is Tomorrow the Day This Giro Explodes? | Giro d'Italia 2026
item.description: Johan Bruyneel and Spencer Martin break down Alberto Bettiol’s beautiful win in Verbania along the shores of Lake Maggiore, discussing how Bettiol used his experience and class to beat his breakaway companions, and what this means for both Bettiol and his XDS-Astana team. They also look ahead to tomorrow’s crucial Stage 14, giving their picks for the win and explaining why they think it is the most important GC stage yet. item.title: Absolute Masterclass! And a Look Ahead to Stage 14 | Giro d'Italia 2026 Stage 13 Podcast
item.description: Stage 13 of the 2026 Giro d'Italia showed that patience beat power on the final climb. A frustrated Filippo Ganna could not get into the break in his hometown. A 15-rider breakaway spent most of the stage out front, and when Leknessund attacked first on the final climb it looked like the move of the day. But Bettiol waited, saw the rider ahead go into the red, and launched right at the top. Perfect timing. Three wins from three different riders for Astana. Behind them, the peloton was happy to leave the GC battle on hold before the monster that is Stage 14, which is also previewed in this episode. Ned Boulting and Jacopo Guarnieri break it all down. For The Love Of Cycling is presented by Science in Sport. This episode of For The Love Of Cycling is sponsored by Bikmo cycle insurance. Protect your ride before it is too late. item.title: Perfectly Timed Attack! | Giro d'Italia 2026 Stage 12 Podcast
item.description: Stage 12 of the 2026 Giro d'Italia was raced hard by the peloton. No one was giving the sprinters an easy day. Milan and Magnier were forced to watch their chances slip away as the race stayed aggressive. Ben Turner had to fight his way back after a puncture at the worst possible moment. Ciccone attacked from 7 kilometres out, desperate for a stage win, but the peloton would not let him go. Then Alec Segaert attacked with 3 kilometres to go, with nobody able or willing to follow. It was a perfectly timed move that left everyone else looking at each other. Jonathan Milan is running out of chances to win fast. Ned Boulting and Jacopo Guarnieri break it all down. For The Love Of Cycling is presented by Science in Sport. This episode of For The Love Of Cycling is sponsored by Bikmo cycle insurance. Protect your ride before it is too late.
