item.title: A Picture-Perfect Attack and the GC Tension Builds | Giro d'Italia 2026
item.description: Johan Bruyneel and Spencer Martin break down Alec Segaert's perfectly timed late attack to win Stage 12 over a depleted peloton, after Movistar blew up the race on a mid-stage climb. They look at how Segaert read the race perfectly, how the pieces came together for his winning move, and what to make of his GC rivals allowing race leader Afonso Eulálio to take six bonus seconds, with Ben O'Connor getting four, on the run-in to the finish. They also look ahead to tomorrow's Stage 13, giving their picks for the win and whether they expect the stage to be won from the breakaway or the peloton. item.title: 421: Top Ganna, Narvaez Hat Trick, Jonas in Pink on Saturday?
item.description: Filippo Ganna crushed the opposition in Tuesday's individual time trial at the Giro d'Italia, riding the fastest time trial over 40 km in a Grand Tour at nearly 55 km/h on average. We got three Danes in the top 10, but perhaps surprisingly for some, none of them was Jonas Vingegaard, although he did gain time on Felix Gall and nearly erased all of Afonso Eulálio's lead. That makes it look as if Vingegaard could take the pink leader's jersey on Saturday's short but hard mountain stage. Otherwise, the Giro's big story is Jhonatan Narvaez, who on Wednesday completed his hat trick with another stage win in this year's first Grand Tour. Narvaez is also among the favorites on Friday, when the Giro continues on the road toward Rome. Veloropa Podcast is presented by Bygma (win a Jonas Vingegaard jersey and helmet at bygma.dk/visma-partner), betting provider Betano (betano.dk), and electronics chain Power (avis.power.dk).

item.title: How Is a Shorthanded UAE Dominating This Race? | Giro d'Italia 2026
item.description: Johan Bruyneel and Spencer Martin break down Jhonatan Narváez's impressive stage win over Enric Mas from the breakaway, giving him his third win of this Giro and UAE their fourth stage victory since Stage 4. They look at how the winning move formed, how Narváez played his cards perfectly, and what we learned from the GC group behind. They also preview Stage 12, sharing their picks for the win and whether they expect any teams to try to shake the sprinters loose on the mid-stage climbs. item.title: Tactical Battle! Narvaez vs Mas | Giro d'Italia 2026 Stage 11 Podcast
item.description: Stage 11 of the 2026 Giro d'Italia and Jhonatan Narvaez can't stop winning. A star-studded breakaway went up the road: Ulissi, Narvaez, Mas, Harper, but in the end it came down to a two-man tactical battle. Enric Mas knew Narvaez was the faster sprinter, so he tried to drop him and couldn't. Narvaez read every move, matched every acceleration, and took his third stage win of this Giro, and his team's fourth. Eulálio gets to enjoy a seventh night in the maglia rosa. With two sprint stages still to come, will he keep it even longer? Ned Boulting and Jacopo Guarnieri break it all down. For The Love Of Cycling is presented by Science in Sport. item.title: Did the Giro's GC Battle Just Get Even More Interesting? | Giro d'Italia 2026
item.description: Johan Bruyneel and Spencer Martin break down Tuesday's long individual time trial, which was won in dominant fashion by Filippo Ganna, with his teammate Thymen Arensman finishing second and moving up to third overall. The pair discuss race leader Afonso Eulálio holding onto the Maglia Rosa despite a poor day, and what to make of Jonas Vingegaard's underwhelming ride, which saw him fail to distance Felix Gall as much as expected. They also look ahead to Stage 11, giving their picks for the win and whether they expect any GC action on the tough parcours in the second half of the stage. item.title: Ganna's Perfect TT and Jonas Closes in on Pink | Giro d'Italia 2026 Stage 10 Podcast
item.description: Stage 10 of the 2026 Giro d'Italia and the race against the clock delivered. A 42-kilometre individual time trial from Viareggio to Massa, perfectly designed for Filippo Ganna. He did not disappoint. It was a ride of absolute precision and power from the Italian, on a course that suited every strength he has. But Ganna wasn't the only Ineos rider to shine. Thymen Arensman put in a ride that proved once again Netcompany-INEOS are among the very best when it comes to time trialling. In the GC fight, Jonas Vingegaard gained time on Gall but lost time to Arensman, and Eulálio put in another courageous effort to defend. So where does that leave the race for pink? Ned Boulting and Jacopo Guarnieri break down a stage that was about more than just the winner. For The Love Of Cycling is presented by Science in Sport. item.description: Anders Mielke spent the second rest day of the Giro with the Decathlon guys, Rasmus Søjberg Pedersen and Tord Gudmestad. An episode that makes it impossible not to smile.

item.title: Is the Marcel Kittel comeback on?
item.description: Nine stages into the Giro, the race has delivered chaos, crashes, and near misses. Marcel Kittel joins us on the rest day from the Unibet Rose Rockets camp to break down how the team has handled its first Grand Tour test, why stage 6 hurt so much, and what it really takes to build a sprint train capable of mixing with the best. Marcel is now on the other side of the race, guiding the sprint group from the team car, and he walks us through the emotions of the Giro so far: the broken bus window in Naples, Dylan and Elmar’s crash in the final corner of stage 6, and why Unibet can still leave with a stage win despite the chances slipping away. We also dive into the details behind a Grand Tour lead-out, why alignment, roles, and 30-page briefings matter, how Wout Poels could still find opportunities in the harder stages, and what Marcel has learned in his new role as a performance leader rather than a rider.

item.title: S14 Ep43: The Dukes of Hazard
item.description: Long-time regulars on The Cycling Podcast’s Giro d’Italia coverage, Matt White and Max Sciandri are directing operations together from the front seat of the Movistar team car in this year’s race. Australia and Italy, fire and ice, they make an intriguing duo, but can they be successful together at this year’s Giro? We went behind the scenes on the day of their boldest tactical plan yet, on stage 4 of the Giro to Cosenza, to get a sense of how their partnership is galvanizing the Spanish team. Daniel Friebe then summoned Matt and Max the following morning for a debrief and a typically cheerful discussion of what they each bring to their roles. item.title: Giro Rest Day Reflections 2026 Stages 4
item.description: Matt Stephens is back on Breakaway duty for another week of racing from the TNT Sports / HBO Max studio, and for the first time ever at an external broadcast featuring a live studio audience at the Sigma Sports store in Hampton Wick. It wouldn’t be Matt Stephens without some unpredictable tangents, and this week’s spanner in the works comes in the shape of a fractured metatarsal. Brave Matt soldiers on to tell us about Thai curries, Toblerones, the merits of automatic vehicles while sporting an injured left foot, and of course a full week of action-packed Grand Tour racing! www.sigmasports.com/ Learn more about your ad choices. item.title: How many stages will UAE Team Emirates win in the second week?
item.description: Jonas Vingegaard finally made his move on Blockhaus, with Felix Gall the only rider who came close to following him and Giulio Pellizzari paying the price for going too deep, too early. We make the case for Eulálio as a genuine podium contender for third place, and discuss why Jai Hindley might be the rider who can stop him. We also dig into the power data from Velon: Vingegaard's three-minute attack on Blockhaus, Paul Magnier's standing-start sprint in Naples, and Giulio Ciccone's stage 9 attack. We also explain why Davide Piganzoli is the most impressive Visma support rider you have probably never heard of. Then we look ahead to a brutal second week. A 42-kilometre time trial from Viareggio to Massa on Tuesday that should give Vingegaard the pink jersey. A run of stages where Lidl-Trek and Soudal Quick-Step will fight for sprint wins. And then Pila on Saturday, 16.6 kilometres at 7.1%, where Gall could realistically take the fight back to Vingegaard. This episode is fuelled by MNSTRY - 100% vegan sports nutrition trusted by Unibet Rose Rockets, CANYON//SRAM zondacrypto and more. Use code DOMESTIQUE20 at mnstry.com for 20% off your tasting box. Order now!

item.title: #105 Gruppetto on Forhjulslir: Book Launch
item.description: Tomorrow, May 19, the book Gruppetto - en hjælperytters kamp by Gruppetto member Mathias Sunekær Norsgaard and author Niclas Sten will be released. In this special episode, we talk about how the book developed from idea to print, the collaboration between Matson and Niclas, and how Niclas, as the author, had to try to put Matson’s voice into writing. You can buy the book at your local bookstore or on the publisher’s website: https://www.lindhardtogringhof.dk/sport/produkt/gruppetto Participants: Anders Mielke, Mathias Sunekær Norsgaard & Niclas Sten

item.title: Should You Keep It in the Candy Drawer or the Fridge?
item.description: First we had Cold or Hot Banana, and now we’re asking how you should have your chocolate: in the candy drawer or in the fridge? We clearly don’t agree. The Giro is, of course, being turned over, but this week’s most important bike race, the Tour of Hungary, is stealing the spotlight. Can we get a podcast with Big Mathi, Krede and Bastian Emil? It somehow has to involve carrier pigeons. That much we know. Jakob has also been browsing DBA and is baffled by the number of soft-serve machine rental ads, while Mads just wants the leftover stock of Bøf Jensen’s favorite sauce. It’s a messy affair, and that’s why everything is as usual in this episode of Lang Distance.

item.title: #1 Giro Diaries
item.description: Nine stages down — any Giro d’Italia blues on the rest day? Join Anders Mielke as he looks back on the opening week of the 2026 Giro d’Italia with colleagues from Eurosport and voices from the peloton and the cycling world. From pink drama to life on the road, this is your audio postcard from Bulgaria and Italy after the first nine stages of the Giro. Follow every stage live on Eurosport and TNT Sports, or stream the Giro on HBO Max.

item.title: Can Felix Gall Actually Beat Vingegaard at This Giro? | Giro d'Italia 2026 Week 1 Review
item.description: Johan Bruyneel and Spencer Martin break down Jonas Vingegaard’s win on Sunday’s summit finish on Stage 9 of the Giro d’Italia, and discuss what to make of Felix Gall’s impressive second place and what it means for the GC battle as the race heads into week two. They also look back at the first nine stages, assess who has overperformed or underperformed, and consider what to expect in Tuesday’s Stage 10 time trial. item.title: Gall vs Vingegaard: Who Had the Legs? | Giro d'Italia Stage 9 Breakdown
item.description: Decathlon controlled the pace of the peloton right up until the final climb that decided everything. It was a valiant effort from the breakaway, with Giulio Ciccone lasting the longest, but behind him Visma and Decathlon smelled blood in the GC fight. Pellizzari and others were dropped as the gradients bit. At the top of the mountain, the two strongest riders battled it out. But the man in pink? He’s not going anywhere just yet. Ned Boulting and Jacopo Guarnieri report from the finish line with all the reaction and analysis. This episode of For The Love Of Cycling is sponsored by Bikmo cycle insurance. item.title: 420: Vingegaard Moves Closer to Pink
item.description: With Sunday’s victory, Jonas Vingegaard is now seriously closing in on the pink leader’s jersey in the Giro d’Italia. He may even take it on Tuesday’s time trial, which comes right after the Giro’s second rest day. Vingegaard appears to be focused on Austrian Felix Gall in the battle for the overall win, and Gall’s Decathlon team launched an attack today, though it did not shake the Danish favorite. Another Dane, Mikkel Bjerg, did huge work when his UAE teammate Jhonatan Narvaez took his second stage win on Saturday. Bjerg is also among the outsiders for the time trial, where Filippo Ganna is the big favorite. item.title: Are UAE Proving You Don’t Need a GC Leader? | Giro d'Italia 2026
item.description: Johan Bruyneel and Spencer Martin break down Jhonatan Narváez’s win on a fast and furious Giro stage, which saw an elite breakaway go clear after an hour-plus battle to get into the move. They discuss how the win highlights Narváez’s often underrated ability, and how UAE losing their GC leader, Adam Yates, has allowed them to show the near-absurd strength and depth of their squad. They also revisit the power numbers behind yesterday’s summit finish, how they show the evolution of the sport over the last few years, and what they expect to happen at tomorrow’s summit finish. Listen in to see what the duo thinks of the stage, how the action will play out tomorrow, who will come out on top, and which riders present the best betting value. item.title: UAE Strike Back: Solo Breakaway Takes Stage 8
item.description: After a terrible start to this Giro, UAE reminded the peloton they’re still here. After a day of repeated attacks and savage gradients on the way into Fermo, one rider finally went alone to take the stage. A brave effort from Leknessund fell short, which meant Jhonatan Narvaez could take his second win of this Giro. Behind them, the GC battle raged on, with the man in pink holding firm and losing nothing on a day that could have reshaped the standings. Ned Boulting and Jacopo Guarnieri report from the finish line with all the reaction and analysis. 🚴 If you’re watching on YouTube, hit the subscribe button for daily Giro d’Italia 2026 coverage This episode of For The Love Of Cycling is sponsored by Bikmo cycle insurance. item.title: Reaction as Jonas Strikes on Blockhaus in the GC Battle! 🇩🇰
item.description: At the freezing summit of Blockhaus, Ned Boulting and Jacopo Guarnieri react to Jonas Vingegaard making a big statement at this year's Giro d'Italia. Felix Gall was able to challenge the race favorite, but Vingegaard held on for his first stage win of the year. Eulalio remains in pink, but for how much longer? Ned and Jacopo also share their tactics for staying warm. This episode of For The Love Of Cycling is sponsored by Bikmo cycle insurance.

item.title: 419: Jonas Conquers Blockhaus
item.description: Jonas Vingegaard won Friday's stage to Blockhaus and strengthened his status as the favorite in this year's Giro d'Italia. The victory may have been a little narrower than many expected, with the Dane taking just 13 seconds on Felix Gall, who currently looks like Vingegaard's biggest challenger. Should Danish cycling fans be worried? That is one of the questions discussed in this episode of Veloropa Podcast, along with a look back at the week's dramatic stages and ahead to the Giro's tough weekend stages before Monday's second rest day. Veloropa Podcast is presented by Betano, Bygma, and Power.

item.description: Johan Bruyneel and Spencer Martin break down Davide Ballerini's unexpected Stage 6 win in the heart of Naples after a late crash on the cobbled finishing straight took down the day's top contenders. They discuss whether the finish was safe, how GC teams are taking safety into their own hands in these sprint finishes, and whether aggressive tire choices may be contributing to crashes on wet days. item.title: Episode 157: Rob Britton, from road pro to gravel star
item.description: This week, James and Will are joined by former road pro turned gravel ace Rob Britton. Born in Saskatchewan, Canada, in 1984, Rob turned pro in 2010 with American team Bissell Pro Cycling and spent the next 12 seasons racing at Continental level. He arguably enjoyed his best years with Rally Cycling (not the Raleigh team, though he did race for that Raleigh in 2013), where he won the Tour of Utah, his second Tour of Gila in New Mexico, and the Canadian National Time Trial title. With strong results in hot, long, multi-day races, Rob was a natural fit for gravel when, like many of his road peers, he made the switch. At first he raced as a privateer, with some kit sponsorship but no real team support. He finished sixth in his first major gravel race, Unbound, in 2022, then won Badlands the following year over 800km and 16,000 vertical metres. In 2023 he took second at Traka behind fellow former road pro Pete Stetina. Arguably his best result so far came when the then 40-year-old beat Lachlan Morton at Unbound XL, finishing the 578km course at an astonishing average speed of 32.4 km/h and breaking the course record by 12 minutes. Those rides helped him become part of the OVRLND adventure team, backed by Easton Cycling, Castelli and Factor Bikes. At this year’s Traka 560, he rode Factor’s radical aero-suspension gravel bike, the Sarana. Whatever bike Rob rides, he makes gruelling long-distance efforts look easy, but as we discuss in this episode, his cycling journey has been anything but easy, shaped by the challenges of racing for smaller teams, the shrinking US race calendar, and reinventing himself as a gravel racer while also finding sponsors and maintaining his own bikes. We caught up with Rob in Italy ahead of racing at The Hills Gravel Race in Treviso in March. Chat begins at 6:54. ----------------- This episode is brought to you by the Hammerhead Karoo GPS bike computer. Visit hammerhead.io and use the code CYCLIST to get a free HR strap with every purchase (just be sure to add the strap to your cart, then apply the code at checkout). item.title: Wrong Turns, Crashes, and Comebacks: Arrieta vs. Eulálio
item.description: It’s over, it’s not over, it’s over again, it’s not over again! A soaking wet day in Italy made every kilometre a fight. Giulio Ciccone gave everything in a brave solo effort to save his maglia rosa, but that wasn’t what Lidl-Trek had in mind. Then came the two-man fight for the stage. Igor Arrieta and Afonso Eulálio went head-to-head, both hitting the deck. One went the wrong way, looked done, and then clawed his way back into contention. The Giro d'Italia doesn’t disappoint. Ned Boulting and Jacopo Guarnieri report from the finish line with all the reaction and analysis. This episode of For The Love Of Cycling is sponsored by Bikmo cycle insurance. item.title: Cykelnerven: Mountain Biking for a Greater Cause
item.description: Once again this year, 600 Danish recreational riders are ready to conquer the toughest Tour de France mountains as part of Cykelnerven. The concept raises vital funds every year for the Danish Multiple Sclerosis Society’s research into multiple sclerosis, and well-known ambassadors such as Dennis Ritter, Christian Moberg and Tom Kristensen join the mountain rides each year in support of the cause. In this episode, you can learn more about Cykelnerven when Andreas Juul Ingvartsen and Søren Kuipers join Bagerstop’s microphones. Andreas Juul Ingvartsen is the event manager for Cykelnerven, while Søren Kuipers is an avid recreational cyclist and writer for altomcykling.dk. Support the show

item.title: Movistar Lights the Fuse as a Few Favorites Scramble | Giro d'Italia 2026
item.description: Johan Bruyneel and Spencer Martin break down a furious day of racing that saw Movistar blow up the race on a mid-stage climb, only for UAE's Jhonatan Narváez to swoop in and win the stage. They discuss how the Giro d'Italia stage win is a huge moment for Narváez, who recently returned from a long recovery after a bad early-season crash, whether Ineos made the right decision in sending stage contender Ben Turner back to pace a dropped Egan Bernal, and how Visma and Jonas Vingegaard looked unshakeable near the front all day. They then preview Wednesday's Stage 5, a tough mountain stage that could bring GC action. Listen in to hear what the duo thinks of the stage, how the action will unfold tomorrow, who they expect to come out on top, and which riders offer the best betting value. item.title: Movistar Set a Blistering Pace on the First Big Climb | Giro d'Italia Stage 4 Breakdown
item.description: For the first time, the peloton raced on Italian soil, and Movistar made sure it hurt. A blistering pace on the first real climb of this Giro shredded the bunch, with riders getting dropped left and right, including the maglia rosa. But the biggest story of the day? Egan Bernal was dropped. What does this mean for his Giro ambitions? How much did Movistar's aggression reshape the general classification? And who capitalized on the chaos? Ned Boulting and Jacopo Guarnieri report from the finish line with all the reaction and analysis. This episode of For The Love Of Cycling is sponsored by Bikmo cycle insurance. item.title: #104 Gruppettoen on Forhjulslir: Egholm Special
item.description: In this episode of Gruppettoen on Forhjulslir, Matson sits down with young Kristian Egholm, who will pull on the Lidl-Trek jersey tomorrow for the Tour of Hungary. Five stages. WorldTour atmosphere. A real taste of the world most kids dream about. They talk about the next step in his career, his dreams, and the strange feeling of suddenly lining up alongside riders he used to watch on TV just a few years ago. This is not a journalistic interview, just an honest conversation the day before it all begins. Featuring: Mathias Sunekær Norsgaard and Kristian Egholm.

item.title: S1 Ep696: Does Gravel Racing Have a Cheating Problem? | The GCN Show Ep. 696
item.description: This week on The GCN Show, we dive into the controversial world of gravel and ultra-endurance racing following allegations of course-cutting and illegal support at major European events. We also investigate the mysterious sabotage of Ollie Bridgewood’s bike at the Fred Whitton Challenge and take a look at the brand-new Canyon Endurace models. Plus, we celebrate a father-son duo breaking three world records and catch up on the latest racing results from the Giro d'Italia and the Women's Vuelta.

item.title: Inside Decathlon's Giro with Rasmus Søjberg Pedersen
item.description: Three stages into the Giro, the race is already wide open. Rasmus Søjberg joins us on the rest day to break down what we've seen so far: the crashes, the sprint margins, and why UAE could still win stages despite losing key riders. Rasmus is one of the lead-out men for Tobias Lund Andersen at Decathlon CMA CGM, and he walks us through what went right and what went wrong on stages 1 and 3, why Milan looked off the pace, and how Magnier has put himself in the conversation as a top-three sprinter in this race. We also look ahead to stage 4, the Blockhaus on stage 7, the time trial, and Felix Gall's GC ambitions. If you want to understand how a Grand Tour really works from inside the team bus, this is the conversation.

item.title: UAE Fall Apart! What Happens Next at the Giro?
item.description: What on earth do UAE do now? On the first rest day, Ned Boulting sits down with David Millar and Pete Kennaugh to make sense of it all. They discuss UAE Team Emirates' dwindling GC options after a nightmare crash, which stages Jonas Vingegaard is likely to target for his big move, what's been going on with Jonathan Milan, and why Paul Magnier has been one of the most exciting stories of the race so far. The guys also have a much-needed catch-up about what they've all been up to over the past few weeks. This episode of For The Love Of Cycling is sponsored by Bikmo cycle insurance. item.title: 418: Crashes and a Pink Comet
item.description: The inbox is on fire after last week's quiz disaster, with a full-on listener and viewer backlash. Will things go better this time when Plesner and Djurhuus try to guess the latest Giro stage winner in Naples? The Giro is moving from the first three stages in Bulgaria to Italy, where Jonas Vingegaard will try to put his rivals to the sword on Friday on the Blockhaus climb. Vingegaard tested his legs on stage 2 in Bulgaria, which was marked by a huge crash that took down most of the UAE squad. The stage was sensationally won by Thomas Silva from Uruguay, who also takes the pink leader's jersey to Italy. The other two stages in Bulgaria were won by Paul Magnier, who first outfoxed Tobias Lund and then Jonathan Milan in the sprint. item.title: Giro Rest Day Reflections 2026 Stages 1-3: Drama, Ducks, and Smooth Sprinting
item.description: This year, Matt Stephens is more nomadic than ever, as he presents his Rest Day Reflections podcast mini-series from the TNT Sports / HBO Max studio, in-store at Sigma Sports, and from the Giro d'Italia itself. In this episode, Matt looks back at stages 1-3 of the 2026 Giro d'Italia as it kicked off in Bulgaria. Exciting finishes, an almost untimely end for a Stockley Park duck, and of course those two horror crashes in stages 1 and 2 all feature. With cameos from Adam Blythe and Orla Chennoui, there are plenty of laughs along the way too. Learn more about your ad choices. Visit podcastchoices.com/adchoices

item.title: Giro Rest Day Reflections 2026 Stages 1-3: Drama, Ducks and Smooth Sprinting
item.description: This year, Matt Stephens is more nomadic than ever, presenting his Rest Day Reflections podcast mini-series from the TNT Sports / HBO Max studio, in-store at Sigma Sports, and from the Giro d’Italia itself. item.title: What Have We Learned After Three Days?
item.description: Three stages in Bulgaria, three very different stories. Paul Magnier wins the opening stage in Burgas and takes the first maglia rosa of his career, then doubles up in Sofia. Guillermo Thomas Silva becomes the first Uruguayan ever to win a Grand Tour stage, taking pink into Italy. And on a slippery road with 23 km to go, a mass crash takes Marc Soler, Jay Vine, Adam Yates and Santiago Buitrago out of the race in a single moment. In the middle of the carnage, Jonas Vingegaard launches a sharp double attack on the Lyaskovets Monastery, and only Giulio Pellizzari can respond. We dig into the Velon power data, including Vingegaard’s 520 watts for a minute and a half, Magnier’s stage 1 sprint with 300 watts less peak power than Jonathan Milan, and the remarkably easy first three days, where Adam Yates averaged 150 watts for 100 km. We also discuss the stage 1 finale that should never have been designed that way, the broken-helmet question around rider safety, and whether cycling needs G-force sensors in helmets. Then we look ahead to the Italian week: stage 4 to Cosenza, the punchy day to Potenza, Naples on cobbles, and the big one on Friday. It’s 244 km to Blockhaus, the longest mountain stage in over a decade and the race’s first true GC test. This episode is fueled by MNSTRY. item.title: S14 Ep36: Arrivée: Vuelta Femenina
item.description: There’s plenty of racing and plenty of Spanish omelettes for Rose Manley and Rebecca Charlton to dig into in this special Arrivée episode on the Vuelta Femenina. Over seven days of racing, from the fairly bumpy to the steeply mountainous, including a historic first summit finish on the Angliru, there was no shortage of drama in the first Grand Tour of the year. With back-to-back champion Demi Vollering not competing this year, a new champion was guaranteed, but who would it be? Would Tour de France Femmes champion Pauline Ferrand-Prévot confirm her Grand Tour credentials in Spain? Could Anna van der Breggen show that she is back to her best in stage racing? Or would this be the breakthrough moment for a surprising young star? Also on the agenda: free jazz race reportage, unwanted gifts, and Denny Gray’s 4am McDonald’s order. item.title: Dramatic Sprint Finish! | Giro d'Italia 2026 Stage 1 Breakdown
item.description: Who better to hear from than the two commentators who watched every second of the stage from the best seat in the house? Ned Boulting and Jacopo Guarnieri share insight from inside the commentary box, just moments after a thrilling Stage 1 sprint at the Giro d'Italia. They break down a strong breakaway effort from kilometre 0, a chaotic lead-out from 5 kilometres out, a nasty crash inside the final few hundred metres, and a captivating three-man sprint to the line. Listen in to hear how the duo thinks the stage will unfold, who could come out on top, and which riders offer the best betting value. item.title: 417: The Big Giro Preview
item.description: It’s time to take a close look at the Giro’s biggest favorites, fast men, outsiders, strong climbers, and not least a record number of Danish riders. Here you get a rundown of all 23 teams and their biggest names, with extra focus on Jonas Vingegaard’s Visma squad - and perhaps a few dark horses worth watching when the race starts on Friday. We also look at the first three stages in Bulgaria and at who the favorites are for the points and mountains jerseys in this year’s Giro d’Italia. item.title: Grand Tour Debutants in the Air
item.description: Forhjulslir is presented in partnership with Continental Dæk Danmark. Continental is the most successful tire in the WorldTour peloton, from Tadej Pogačar in the rainbow jersey to Tobias Lund and Filippo Ganna in the Giro. Safety starts with the tire - both on the highway and on the bike path. Fly to Bulgaria and the start of this year’s Giro d’Italia with the Danish Grand Tour debutants Rasmus Søjberg Pedersen and Johan Price-Pejtersen. We begin in Ryanair’s emergency exit row with Søjberg, then move 45 minutes into the episode up to business class with Price. What do you pack for your first Grand Tour? Which books come along? What will their roles be, and how nervous are they? The two debutants get you fully ready for the Giro start. Featuring Rasmus Søjberg Pedersen, Johan Price-Pejtersen and Anders Mielke.

item.title: S1 Ep695: Teenager Paul Seixas Is Racing the Tour de France, But Is It a Good Idea?
item.description: In this week’s GCN Show, we discuss the bombshell news that 19-year-old French phenomenon Paul Seixas is set to make his debut at this year’s Tour de France. We also dive into the latest tech from The Traka, including a first look at the new Campagnolo Record 13 Wireless groupset and prototype gravel bikes from Canyon and Factor. Plus, we look at Tadej Pogačar’s new "gym bro" physique and the growing controversy around e-bike bans on outdoor trails.

item.title: How to Thrive on the Bike After 40 with Marissa Axell
item.description: Marissa Axell is a Cat 1 cyclist and cycling coach with more than 15 years of experience, and she has dedicated her work to helping women over 40 ride stronger, train smarter, and stop feeling like their best days on the bike are behind them. In this episode, Marissa shares the training mistakes she sees women over 40 make most often, how to balance strength work, skills training, and recovery without it taking over your life, and the mental toughness tools she uses with her athletes to get through the hard stuff. 🔥 Shop Femme Cyclist training plans to help you achieve your biggest goals on the bike: https://shop.femmecyclist.com/product-category/training-plans/ Full Show Notes: https://femmecyclist.com/marissa-axell-interview ❤️ Join Kristen & The Femme Cyclist Community ➡︎ Website: https://femmecyclist.com ➡︎ YouTube: https://www.youtube.com/femmecyclist ➡︎ Instagram: https://instagram.com/femme_cyclist 💜 Connect With Marissa Axell 👉 Instagram: @marissa.axell 👉 Website: axnfitnessandcoaching.com Support the show 👉Instagram: @femme_cyclist 👉Website: https://femmecyclist.com

item.title: S14 Ep32: 2026 Giro d’Italia
item.description: On Friday, the first Grand Tour of the season rolls out from Bulgaria, not Italy - and, as always, we’ll be there on the ground to bring you all the action from the Giro d’Italia. This year’s Corsa Rosa starts here, with our race preview and the trio who’ll take you on the journey to Rome over the next three weeks: Daniel Friebe, Brian Nygaard and Michele Pelacci. Before we sink our teeth into the Giro, though, we discuss the first big headline of the week: Paul Seixas’s announcement that he’ll be riding the Tour de France. Then it’s time to talk about the Giro, first focusing on the favorites, then moving on to the beautiful canvas on which Jonas Vingegaard and others will hopefully create cycling art over the next three weeks. item.title: What Are Bahrain Victorious' Giro Plans? ft. Bahrain's Director Sportif
item.description: In this episode, Gorazd Štangelj joins Aidan in the Hotseat for a look inside the role of a sports director, from preseason planning and race tactics to the split-second decisions made from the team car. Gorazd explains why modern racing has become faster and more chaotic, with positioning now often deciding races before the climbs even begin. He reflects on his own Giro history, the race’s unique demands, and why experience with Italian roads, weather, and racing culture still matters. The conversation then turns to Bahrain Victorious’ Giro d’Italia plans. Santiago Buitrago leads the team for GC, but Štangelj makes clear that Bahrain will also hunt stages through riders such as Damiano Caruso, Afonso Eulálio, Alex Segaert and Edoardo Zambanini. He also breaks down the difference between racing against Visma and UAE, where small tactical windows can open, and why Bahrain’s targets are a strong GC finish and an early stage win to lift the whole team. The episode closes with thoughts on bonus seconds, the white jersey, young talents like Paul Seixas, and the relentless post-stage routine of a Grand Tour sports director.

item.title: Sprint Like a Rocket with Marcel Kittel
item.description: In episode two of Half Wheeling, we’re joined by sprint legend Marcel Kittel. Now back in the peloton as the sprint coach for Unibet Rose Rockets, he reflects on his early retirement and his move into a completely new role. Kittel shares what it really takes to return to the sport and why this project pulled him back in. At the heart of the episode is his work with Dylan Groenewegen. Former rivals turned partners, we dive into Groenewegen’s comeback this season and the dreams of a stage win or even the pink jersey at the upcoming Giro d'Italia. Sharp insights, honest reflections, and a front-row seat to one of cycling’s most intriguing comebacks. Host: Anders Mielke. The Giro d'Italia kicks off on Friday. Catch all the pink drama on Eurosport and TNT Sports, or stream it live on HBO Max.

item.title: Episode 416: The Great Giro Route Special
item.description: It’s almost time for the first Grand Tour of the year, the Giro d’Italia, where Danish rider Jonas Vingegaard is the overwhelming favorite. In this preview episode, we focus mainly on the route, and especially on the different landscapes and regions the Giro riders will cross over the next three weeks starting this Friday. For the occasion, we’ve been lucky enough to bring Giro aficionado and Italy lover Michael Olrik-Andersen into the studio. We also look at the latest news and results in cycling, from Tadej Pogačar’s historic win in the Tour of Romandie to today’s striking announcement that rising star Paul Seixas will start this year’s Tour de France. The Veloropa Podcast is presented in collaboration with Bygma, the largest Danish-owned supplier to the construction industry, always stocking the latest Airtox on the shelves (bygma.dk), betting provider Betano (betano.dk), and electronics chain Power (avis.power.dk).

item.description: This episode can be watched for free on YouTube. Go and subscribe to @eurosportdanmark now. On Friday, May 8, it all kicks off in Bulgaria. The stage is set for a huge Giro drama, with Jonas Vingegaard trying to win his third Grand Tour. Michael Mørkøv and Brian Holm get you dressed in pink.

item.title: The Giro d'Italia Preview!
item.description: The 109th Giro d'Italia starts this Friday in Bulgaria - three weeks, 3,459 kilometres, and 50,000 metres of climbing all the way to Rome. Jonas Vingegaard is the clear favorite, chasing the third Grand Tour of his career and the treble that would put him alongside Merckx, Hinault, Anquetil, Gimondi, Contador, Nibali, and Froome. We break down the route stage by stage - the Bulgarian Grande Partenza, Blockhaus on stage 7, the 40-kilometre Tuscan time trial, the five-star showdown at Pila, and the Dolomite double of Piani di Pezzè and Piancavallo that will decide the pink jersey. Then the GC favorites: Giulio Pellizzari carrying the hopes of an entire country, Adam Yates stepping into UAE’s leadership role, Derek Gee returning to where it all began, Egan Bernal closer than he has been in years, Thymen Arensman, Jai Hindley, Felix Gall, and the question of who can actually push Vingegaard. This episode is fueled by MNSTRY. Get 20% off your first Tasting Box order with code DOMESTIQUE20 to see why your sports nutrition shouldn’t be a choice between on-bike performance and off-bike health. Order now!

item.title: S14 Ep31: Girovino!! | The Wines of the 2026 Giro
item.description: Every year, as the Giro d’Italia approaches, we ease into our coverage with an episode that has — ostensibly — nothing to do with racing bikes. Any listeners with a passing interest in wine, stay with us. Everyone else, there are plenty of other episodes in our build-up to the Grande Partenza that will be more up your strada. The Cycling Podcast has been on the ground at every Giro since 2016, and the lesson we keep relearning is that these races can’t really be separated from the places that host them. The culture, the land, the things it grows and makes — all of it is part of the story. It was with that in mind that we headed to D Vine Cellars HQ in London last week for the unveiling of our 2026 Girovagando selection (link to buy and list of wines in the shownotes below), with Greg Andrews the master of ceremonies. item.title: Don't Touch Franske!
item.description: Despite a tight schedule, we still managed to get on the phone and go over the big and small things since last time. To be honest, mostly the small things. Skejl’s near miss in Liège, a touching message from 12-year-old Konrad, Mads’s new dandy collection, a big turnout from Preben at Onsdagsræs, merch updates, and what’s next for Mads and Don’t Touch Franske. That and much more in this episode of Lang Distance.

item.title: Pogačar cruises, Godon keeps winning, and Roglič fades in Romandie
item.description: Spencer Martin and Johan Bruyneel review the opening stages of the Tour de Romandie, where Tadej Pogačar and Dorian Godon have each won two stages, along with other races and news, including Ineos' announcement of a partnership with Danish tech company Netcompany. They break down Pogačar's performances so far, discuss whether he is underperforming or overperforming relative to expectations, praise Florian Lipowitz's strong ride behind him, and speculate about what is happening with his Red Bull-Bora-Hansgrohe teammate Primož Roglič. item.title: Pavel Bittner: "The respect goes out the window"
item.description: Pavel Bittner joins the Hot Seat to talk about a brutal Paris-Roubaix campaign, what it's like watching the race from inside the broom wagon, and why he keeps returning to a race that, in his own words, has "always treated me pretty shit." We also discuss his breakthrough win at the Vuelta a España, when he beat Wout van Aert in a sprint at 21 years old, the unspoken etiquette of the final 3 km ("the respect goes out the window"), and where he sees his career heading - sprinter, classics rider, or both. Plus: the truth about Gravel Bittner, his tattoos, and his goals for the rest of 2026, including the Tour de France.

item.title: Pavel Bittner: "The respect goes out the window"

Plus: the truth about Gravel Bittner, his tattoos, and his goals for the rest of 2026, including the Tour de France.

item.title: S14 Ep30: Ineos Turns to AI to Get the Pulse Racing
item.description: The spring Classics campaign has come to an end and the Giro d'Italia is just around the corner. To bridge the gap between the two, and with Daniel Friebe absent as he prepares for the Giro, Lionel Birnie is joined by Tudor Pro Cycling's Larry Warbasse to wrap up the first part of the season, look ahead to the first Grand Tour of the year, and react to major sponsorship news that may help Ineos Grenadiers challenge for the Tour de France again. In part one, Larry updates us on the Tour of the Alps, where he and many riders were fine-tuning their form ahead of the Giro, and one of his teammates made a rapid and successful transition from speed skating to elite-level cycling with a stage win. He also talks about his version of the all-day breakfast and indoor heat training. In the second part, Lionel reports back from the big reveal confirming a new title sponsor for Ineos Grenadiers. From the Giro d'Italia, they will be Netcompany-Ineos thanks to backing from a Danish IT company investing a figure thought to be around €100 million over five years. The target is for the team to win an eighth Tour de France in the next five years, but it is the introduction of Netcompany's AI software, Pulse, that makes this more than just a sponsorship deal. Pulse, which is used at several major airports, including Heathrow, will be used to help the team make better decisions more often. We ask whether its use can extend beyond logistics and training and start to influence racing strategy and tactics. In the final part, we tie up the loose ends from the spring and turn our attention to the Giro before handing over to Daniel and co for daily coverage of the Corsa Rosa, which starts in Bulgaria on Friday, May 8. Image courtesy of Netcompany-Ineos. item.title: 156. Guy Martin: Getting kidnapped, land speed records, and defying death on two wheels
item.description: This week, James is joined by Guy Martin, former British motorcycle racer, land speed record holder, part-time cyclist, and reluctant TV presenter - he says he'd rather be known for his day job fixing trucks than as a TV personality. But on television he is, where you may have seen him building his own narrowboat using archaic methods, breaking tractor land speed records (spoiler: 135 mph), rebuilding First World War tanks, and travelling the globe in the Our Guy in ... series, where Guy visits the Chernobyl reactor in Ukraine, cycles across the desert in China, travels through India on a motorcycle, and much more - we haven't even got to the drug cartels in Colombia yet. Guy is also a keen cyclist, from enduro mountain biking to breaking the British land speed record on a special steel road bike, to his own self-styled ultra-cycling challenges, although he'd just call them 'long rides to get me head straight'. He is also an author. His new book, All the Medals Have Been Handed Out, is out on 7 May, and along with charting his adventures and misadventures on two wheels, it recounts being kidnapped in Colombia and waterboarded; his soul-crushing, death-defying bicycle ride to Azerbaijan; and his continued '300 miles per hour job', an ongoing project to turn a street bike he bought for £5,000 into a record-breaking motorcycle that he'll ride at over 300 mph. And he's tightened all those nuts and bolts himself. We talk about all this and more, so buckle up your four-point harness and drop the clutch. Or just put the kettle on. Chat begins at 5:36. Guy's new book, All the Medals Have Been Handed Out (Penguin Books), is out on 7 May. More details plus where to buy can be found here. item.title: 415: The King and the Crown Prince Dueling in Liège
item.description: Put on your Gaudi helmet, because this episode of Veloropa Podcast covers a lot of ground. From the uncrowned king of modern cycling, Tadej Pogacar, and the 19-year-old crown prince, Paul Seixas, who duelled on La Redoute on Sunday, to Netcompany's entry into cycling, Gaudi art on Visma jerseys, the Tour of the Alps, the Tour de Romandie, where Albert Philipsen is developing as a climber, Jonas Vingegaard's new Bygma helmet, betting tips for Eschborn-Frankfurt, the latest Plesner Power Ranking, and a quiz about Grand Prix Herning itself. Veloropa Podcast is presented in collaboration with Bygma (bygma.dk), the largest Danish-owned supplier to the construction industry, which always has the latest Airtox on the shelves, betting provider Betano (betano.dk), and electronics chain Power (avis.power.dk).

item.title: Pogi vs. Paul and Giro Preview
item.description: Forhjulslir is presented in collaboration with Continental Dæk Danmark. Continental is the most successful tire in the WorldTour peloton, from Tadej Pogačar in the Monuments to Paul Seixas in the Ardennes. Safety starts with the tire - both on the highway and the bike path. The Ardennes Classics are over and Paul Seixas made a strong impression. The young Frenchman did something no one has managed in the past two years: follow Tadej Pogacar when he attacked on La Redoute in Liège-Bastogne-Liège. We dive into LBL, Pogačar's dominance, and Seixas' breakthrough. Then we turn our attention to the Giro d'Italia, where Jonas Vingegaard is chasing the maglia rosa and a major place in cycling and sporting history. Featuring Anders Mielke, Emil Mielke Vinjebo, and Per Bausager.

item.title: Giro d'Italia Preview: Vingegaard, the Stages, and a Podium Prediction
item.description: Only one box remains unchecked on Jonas Vingegaard's Grand Tour belt: the Giro d'Italia. Maybe it will happen this year, with the Danish superstar lining up in the Italian stage race for the first time. Tobias Lund Andresen has also arrived, and the episode's two experts, Christian Moberg Jørgensen and Mathias Mundbjerg, have high expectations for him. This is episode 1 of 2, focusing on Vingegaard's chances and the most exciting of the 21 stages. Support the show

item.title: S1 Ep694: Is the World’s Most Bike-Friendly Country About to Make a Huge Mistake?
item.description: This week on the GCN Show, we look at the surprising rise in cycling accidents in the Netherlands and ask whether the world’s safest cycling nation is about to change its stance on helmets. We also explore how Olympic champion Kristen Faulkner built her own AI system to optimize performance, and take a look at Tadej Pogačar’s brand-new Colnago TT bike. Win a Wahoo ELEMNT BOLT 3 GPS head unit! 👉 https://gcn.eu/GCNWahooCompetition

item.title: Are Pogačar’s Days Numbered?
item.description: Tadej Pogačar won Liège-Bastogne-Liège for the fourth time, his third straight victory there, taking his Monument tally to thirteen. But the story of Sunday was Paul Seixas. The 19-year-old Frenchman became the only rider in history to follow Pogačar on the Côte de la Redoute, matching 8.7 w/kg for nearly four minutes and setting a new climb record in the process. We break down the most extraordinary edition of La Doyenne ever raced, the Remco breakaway controversy, the mystery of Pogačar’s 5,000 Swiss franc jersey fine, and what it all means for the future of the sport. Then we look ahead to the Tour de Romandie, Pogačar’s first stage race of 2026. Florian Lipowitz and Primož Roglič give Red Bull–BORA–hansgrohe their strongest hand, Lenny Martinez tries to improve on last year, and a wave of young talent gets a chance to shine on a route built around the summit finish at Leysin. Recovery is where gains are made, and sleep is the most important part of recovery. MNSTRY developed PRE-SLEEP DRINK with the same WorldTour teams they fuel, including Red Bull-Bora–Hansgrohe and CANYON//SRAM zondacrypto. Hear that and much more in this episode. Guests: Anders Mielke and Mathias Sunekær Norsgaard. Gruppettoen on Forhjulslir is sponsored by Aioss. item.title: Long Distance Can’t Stop Us
item.description: What terrible timing for Jakob’s wifi to drop out, but luckily there’s plenty of bromance when Mads calls Emil Kondrup during this episode. Buzz or not?! The show is full of questions about what happens now that Viktor is no longer with us and where the merch has gone. We also talk about Skjel’s riding in Amstel and Flèche, and have big dreams for Liège. Jakob beat Merch Preben in the sprint, but is officially the worst in the sibling group. We cover a lot in this episode of Lang Distance.

item.title: S14 Ep29: Arrivée: Liège-Bastogne-Liège (Women)
item.description: Get the waffle-o-meter out one last time as Rose Manley and Lizzy Banks react to the final Spring Classic of 2026, Liège-Bastogne-Liège Femmes. With the Ardennes Classics producing both surprise winners and familiar champions so far this season, which way will this tenth edition of Liège-Bastogne-Liège go? Could breakout star Paula Blasi win again this week? Might Demi Vollering claim a record-breaking third title at the race? Or could Kasia Niewiadoma finally take the top step after a season full of podium finishes? Also on the agenda: a picnic’s worth of puns and Preston Bus Station… obviously.

item.title: Liège-Bastogne-Liège 2026 Breakdown
item.description: George Hincapie, Johan Bruyneel, and Spencer Martin break down Tadej Pogačar’s win over teenage sensation Paul Seixas at Liège-Bastogne-Liège, giving him his third straight and fourth career victory at the Ardennes Monument. They discuss Remco Evenepoel’s decision to join an early 50-rider group, which forced UAE to chase hard for more than 100 kilometers, whether a pair of Monument podiums and an Amstel win make this a successful spring for Evenepoel, what Seixas’ rapid improvement means for the future of this rivalry, and whether he should make his Grand Tour debut at the 2026 Tour. item.title: The Fastest Cycling Equipment Right Now
item.description: Which bikes are the fastest right now? Which helmet cuts through the wind best, and which wheel brands perform best in the Silverstone wind tunnel? In this episode of Bagerstop, you can learn more about the fastest cycling equipment right now. You’ll also hear why you might want to look in a different direction, apart from the obvious reason related to personal finances. Aero, cake, and cycling expert Frederik Muff joins the microphone. PS: Note that after recording, it was announced that Galten Bageren has been saved! Support the show

item.title: Can Seixas Dethrone Pogačar at Liège-Bastogne-Liège?
item.description: Spencer Martin and Johan Bruyneel review the final stages of the Tour of the Alps, where Red Bull-Bora-Hansgrohe’s Giulio Pellizzari won the tough final stage and secured the overall title, before previewing Sunday’s showdown between Tadej Pogačar, Remco Evenepoel, and teenage sensation Paul Seixas. They discuss where the decisive move might come and whether anyone can stop Pogačar from claiming a third straight Monument victory. item.title: Pogačar, Evenepoel, or Seixas: Who Will Win Liège-Bastogne-Liège?
item.description: Liège-Bastogne-Liège is the final Monument of the spring. The 259.5 km route through the Ardennes features 11 climbs and one major question: can anyone stop Tadej Pogačar? He is chasing a third straight win, something not achieved since Argentin in 1987, after already winning Strade Bianche, Milan-Sanremo, and the Tour of Flanders this season. Two riders look capable of ending the streak. Paul Seixas, 19, just won La Flèche Wallonne and arrives in top form. Remco Evenepoel, a two-time winner of this race, is racing on home roads with the best Ardennes preparation of his career. We break down the route, the key climbs, the full start list, and give our podium picks.

item.title: “They Stepped Out Into the Road” — Cat Ferguson on Her Most Dramatic Race | Watts Occurring Femmes
item.description: For their final guest of the year, Emma and Manon are joined by four-time World Junior Champion Cat Ferguson. Cat joins them fresh from a very intense team camp as she looks back on her most dramatic race. Listen to “They STEPPED OUT into the road” Cat Ferguson on her most dramatic race | Watts Occurring Femmes by Watts Occurring instantly on your tablet, phone or browser - no downloads needed.

item.title: Women's Cycling Grew 80% Last Year — Here's What's Next ft. Kate Veronneau
item.description: Kate Veronneau joins Aidan Burgess for a wide-ranging conversation covering everything from the moment London secured the 2027 Tour de France Femmes avec Zwift Grand Départ to why Zwift made the painful decision to walk away from Paris-Roubaix Femmes sponsorship. Kate explains why bringing the race to Greater London’s 15 million people dwarfs even Rotterdam’s 650,000 roadside fans, how a random Richmond ride with Maloko Cycling drew nearly 70 people on a weeknight, and why the UK broadcast paywall means even a home Grand Départ may not reach the mainstream audience it deserves. She also talks about coming from basketball, not cycling, and why Zwift has positioned itself as the industry’s “Switzerland” — the one brand that can get SRAM, Shimano, Canyon, and Factor to sit at the same table. From there, the discussion gets more pointed. They examine the 70% viewership spike at Paris-Roubaix Femmes and why the “what the fuck” outrage, in Kate’s words, was the best possible outcome, the 148 million global viewing hours sponsors still are not acting on, and whether women’s cycling should copy the broken men’s model or go fully franchise-based, à la Bas Tietema and Jonathan Vaughters. The episode ends with a preview of the 2026 route through Switzerland, Mont Ventoux, and Nice. Also discussed: the “Watch the Femme” hat, Orla being worth every penny of the TNT paywall, Nike and Red Bull circling, and why Pogačar winning everything is helping women’s cycling enormously.

item.title: S14 Ep27: Arrivée: La Flèche Wallonne (Women)
item.description: Rose Manley is joined by Flèche Wallonne Femmes’ biggest fan, Lizzy Banks, as they react to the 2026 edition. With five former champions on the start line this year, the win could go almost anywhere. And with surprise Amstel Gold Race winner Paula Blasi returning for her second Ardennes Classic, fans may be in for another surprise. Also on the agenda: fickle fan club presidents, cat skinning, and getting your knickers and tongues twisted.

item.title: Seixas Sends a Warning Shot | Flèche Wallonne & Tour of the Alps
item.description: Johan Bruyneel and Spencer Martin break down Paul Seixas’ dominant win at today’s La Flèche Wallonne, which made him the youngest rider in the race’s history and showed he is ready for Sunday’s superstar showdown against Remco Evenepoel and Tadej Pogačar at Liège-Bastogne-Liège. They also review the opening three stages of the ongoing Tour of the Alps, discussing Tom Pidcock’s surprise stage win, the tightly packed GC battle, who they expect to come out on top, and what it means for the upcoming Giro d’Italia. item.title: 414: Three Titans Ready for Liège — Seixas Wins Flèche Debut
item.description: Tadej Pogačar has already won two Monuments this year, Remco Evenepoel beat Mattias Skjelmose at the Amstel Gold Race, and Paul Seixas won La Flèche Wallonne on his debut today. All four will be on the start line for Sunday’s Liège-Bastogne-Liège, the final Monument of the spring, which we preview in this episode. We also discuss a whole string of Danish victories, including Anders Foldager’s international breakthrough when he crossed the line first in the semi-classic Brabantse Pijl. Veloropa Podcast is presented in collaboration with betting provider Betano (betano.dk), the Danish supplement brand Aioss (use code veloropa to get up to 300 kroner off at aioss.dk), and the electronics chain Power (avis.power.dk).

item.title: The Spring of Phenomena
item.description: Forhjulslir is presented in collaboration with Continental Tyres Denmark. Continental is the most successful tire in the WorldTour peloton, from Tadej Pogačar in Roubaix to Paul Seixas in the Ardennes. Safety starts with the tire, both on the highway and on the bike path. We’re finally back together at Bausager Farm. A lot has happened since last time. We look back on what may have been the best spring of this golden era: the Tour of Flanders, Paris-Roubaix, and the cobbles; Amstel, Flèche, and the Ardennes. We recorded this 30 minutes after Paul Seixas’ victory on the Mur de Huy. Can he really beat Tadej Pogačar on Sunday in Liège-Bastogne-Liège? Featuring Anders Mielke and Per Bausager.

item.title: Accepting Pain with Mads Pedersen
item.description: The first episode of Half Wheeling takes a deep dive into Mads Pedersen’s painful comeback. After crashing on the first race day on February 4, 2026, and suffering a broken collarbone and fractured wrist, Pedersen returned against the odds to deliver an extraordinary spring campaign, finishing top 10 in every Classic and top 7 in all three Monuments he contested. This episode unfolds as a documentary-style podcast and montage, with host Anders Mielke following Pedersen and Lidl-Trek from the day after the Tour of Flanders to the defining moment of the spring: Pedersen’s dream race, Paris-Roubaix. Special thanks to Eurosport for race commentary from Paris-Roubaix, and to Lidl-Trek and Mads Pedersen for their access and trust. Host: Anders Mielke. Featuring: Mads Pedersen, Claus Pedersen (father), Mattias Reck (coach), Jeroen Heymans (mechanic), Anthony Lafourte (soigneur), Jens Hinder (doctor), Michael Schär (sports director), Mathias Norsgaard, Mathias Vacek, and Tim Torn Teutenberg (teammates).

item.title: S1 Ep693: The UCI Is Banning Expensive Bikes, and We’re All for It!
item.description: The UCI is taking a stand against the technological escalation of ultra-expensive cycling tech by implementing strict price caps and commercial availability rules for the LA 2028 Olympics. In this week’s GCN Show, Dan and Simon dive into what these new regulations mean for the future of track and road racing. Plus, they discuss Taylor Phinney’s surprise comeback, the results from the Amstel Gold Race, and why President Macron is getting involved in the pro cycling transfer market.

item.title: From World Champion to Wheelchair and Back
item.description: Kait Boyle is a 24-hour World Champion and one of the strongest ultra-endurance mountain bikers in the world. In 2018, just two months after winning the world championship, she survived a life-threatening car accident that left her in a wheelchair, wondering if she would ever ride again. This is her story of coming back, and what she is still chasing. Want to try bikepacking but not sure how to get started? Grab your copy of Saddle Up and Sleep Out: Your Ultimate Guide to Bikepacking. Full show notes: https://femmecyclist.com/kait-boyle-interview. Join Kristen and the Femme Cyclist community on the website, YouTube, Instagram, and Facebook. Connect with Kait Boyle on Instagram, her website, Substack, and Bikepacking Roots. Support the show on Instagram and the website.

item.title: #101 The Gruppetto on Forhjulslir: Byriel Special
item.description: We’re marking a small milestone, and what better way than to go all the way back to the beginning. Rasmus Byriel is back in the studio. We talk about what’s happened since last time: life, cycling, and everything in between. No plan. No filter. One thing we can promise: the original gruppetto vibe is back, just like in the beginning. Tune in. It’s Danish spring. It’s Byriel and Norsgaard. And it’s exactly how it should be.

item.title: Paul Seixas Ready to Rule His First Classic?
item.description: Remco Evenepoel wins the Amstel Gold Race, getting revenge one year after Skjelmose beat him on the line. Bram and Ethan break down how Evenepoel controlled the race without a single flashy attack, and why Skjelmose’s ability to hang on confirms last year was no fluke. They also look at Group 2 syndrome rearing its head again, Evenepoel’s quietly lethal sprint that has now beaten Skjelmose, Van Aert, and Bilbao in similar situations, and why Marco Frigo finishing tenth from the day’s breakaway deserves more attention than it got. From there, the focus shifts to Wednesday’s Flèche Wallonne, which is wide open without Pogačar or Evenepoel. Bram and Ethan run through the contenders for the Mur de Huy, from Seixas and his frightening kick to Grégoire, Lenny Martinez, Cosnefroy, and a dark horse in Daniel Felipe Martinez. They also ask whether Vauquelin can bounce back after his Amstel crash and why Stevie Williams’ continued absence is such a shame. The episode closes with a quick look at the Tour of the Alps, where Pidcock has already finished second on the opening stage and a brutal stage five finale awaits. Giro prep, continental team upsets.

item.title: Viktor Axelsen Special
item.description: Our first-ever special, featuring none other than Viktor Axelsen. We jump straight into a very honest conversation about Viktor’s decision to end his career, taking responsibility and going his own way, why children should keep the joy of sport, uncompromising standards, B.S. coming to Mads’ 35th birthday, whether you learned Chinese on Duolingo, and how often you use cheap homemade fuel. Huge thanks to Viktor for the honesty and the good humor. You’re welcome on Lang Distance any time.

item.title: Amstel Gold 2026 Breakdown
item.description: Lance Armstrong, George Hincapie, Johan Bruyneel, and Spencer Martin break down Remco Evenepoel’s win at Amstel Gold, his first career victory in the race, as he defeats defending champion Mattias Skjelmose. They discuss the key moments of the race and what it means for next weekend’s showdown between Paul Seixas, Tadej Pogačar, and Evenepoel at Liège-Bastogne-Liège. item.title: Benji Naesen: From Gamer to Cycling Expert
item.description: Benji Naesen joins Aidan Burgess for a wide-ranging conversation that starts with how a Pro Cycling Manager disc taped to a magazine got a nine-year-old Belgian hooked on cycling, and goes all the way to weighing 130 kilos and being too ashamed to tell anyone he wanted to change. Benji talks openly about his weight-loss journey, the first time he showed his face on camera and barely slept the night before, and how Zwift gave him a way into riding without giving up his evenings. He also tells the story of how a random DM to Patrick about a Vincenzo Nibali video became the Lantern Rouge Cycling Podcast within days — and why neither of them were surprised when it took off. From there, the discussion gets more heated. They break down the Pogačar problem, why Van der Poel’s Ronde tactics and Wout’s Roubaix approach can’t really be compared, and whether Paul Seixas can genuinely podium the Tour de France or whether expectations for teenagers have become unrealistic. Benji also shares his Tour podium prediction and explains why he thinks Vingegaard’s Giro-Tour double is more about insurance than improvement. The episode ends with a passionate segment on endometriosis awareness, the underfunding of women’s health research, and why Benji thinks cycling needs a RED-S screening system similar to the one used in speed climbing. Also discussed: aerodynamic plastic surgery, koala skinsuits, and why removing a rib for time-trial gains might not be as ridiculous as it sounds.

item.title: Roubaix Recap, Seixas' €8M Question, and Amstel Gold
item.description: Spencer Martin and Johan Bruyneel discuss a few lingering thoughts and questions about Wout van Aert’s incredible win at Paris-Roubaix, as well as the recent rumors of drama between Paul Seixas and his Decathlon-CMA CGM team. They also speculate about whether his agent’s €8 million-per-year asking price is justified. The episode also covers recent racing, including O Gran Camiño and De Brabantse Pijl, before predicting who will win Sunday’s Amstel Gold Race. item.title: Amstel Preview: Can We Already Hand Evenepoel the Trophy?
item.description: The cobbles are done, but new storylines are emerging. As the peloton heads into the 60th edition of the Amstel Gold Race, all eyes are on Remco Evenepoel, who returns after an eye-catching ride at the Tour of Flanders and lines up as the man to beat in the Ardennes opener. Bram and Ethan break it all down: Evenepoel’s chances after his Ronde debut, whether anyone can match him on this terrain, and why Mattias Skjelmose shouldn’t be written off despite a tough week in the Basque Country. They also look at Matteo Jorgenson as Visma’s main card, the depth across teams like Red Bull Bora Hansgrohe, and which outsiders could shake up the race. From wildcard picks like Del Grosso to questions around team tactics and form, the episode covers every angle ahead of Sunday’s race. Expect bold predictions, a few disagreements, and plenty of debate over who actually makes the podium.

item.title: Niels Vandeputte on Winning the Belgian Gravel Championship and Chasing a Cross Jersey
item.description: Belgian cyclocross star Niels Vandeputte joins Matt Stephens to unpack a huge season of 40 races, breakthrough podiums, and the challenge of going head-to-head with legends like Mathieu van der Poel and Wout van Aert. The Alpecin-Premier Tech Development Team rider shares insights into the demands of elite cyclocross racing, how training camps and setbacks shaped his form, and why consistency may be his greatest strength. They also discuss his Belgian gravel championship win, his ambitions on the road, and the long-term goal of earning another coveted championship jersey — this time in cyclocross. www.sigmasports.com Learn more about your ad choices. Visit podcastchoices.com/adchoices

item.title: Monuments: Il Lombardia
item.description: Il Lombardia, the Tour of the Falling Leaves, is a Monument that combines grandeur and beauty in equal measure. With brutal climbs and dizzying descents, charming villages and lush slopes as the backdrop, riders empty the last drops from the season’s cycling fuel tank. In this episode, we take a cable car to the top of the peaks in one of Europe’s most beautiful regions. North of Milan and south of Switzerland, we turn our compass toward the history, legends, and sporting drama of Il Lombardia. Featuring: Jakob Fuglsang, Brian Holm, Bastian Emil Goldschmidt, and Martin Weibel. Support the show

item.title: George Hincapie: Paris-Roubaix, Elbows-Out Racing, and His Modern Adventure
item.description: This week, James and Will chat with George Hincapie, the ultimate domestique, a hard-as-nails Classics rider, and former lieutenant to Lance Armstrong. George was born in Queens, New York City, on 29 June 1973, to cycling-mad Colombian father Ricardo. By age 12, he was throwing elbows while racing adults in Central Park, and by his teens he was showing the talent that would take him to the Olympics in 1992 and his first pro season with Motorola in 1994. What began as a self-confessed bunch of American upstarts became one of the most formidable teams of the 1990s and 2000s: US Postal. There, George established himself as one of cycling’s strongest and most dependable riders, supporting team leader Lance Armstrong as road captain and lieutenant across all seven of Armstrong’s Tour wins — though, of course, those wins are in quotation marks. George retired from the pro peloton in 2012 after 19 seasons, during which he started the Tour de France 17 consecutive times, finished it 16 times, and won one team time trial stage. He also rode 50 Monuments, finishing second at Paris-Roubaix and third at the Tour of Flanders; won Gent-Wevelgem in 2001; and was a three-time US national champion. Today, George is bringing all that experience to his new team, Modern Adventure Pro Cycling, with the goal of giving young riders a shot at the big time while building what he hopes will become 'the next great American team' and one day compete in the Grand Tours. We spoke to George ahead of Paris-Roubaix, where Modern Adventure had the first big wildcard entry of its inaugural season. item.title: What I Wouldn't Do for 2 cm More
item.description: We’re together in person for the first time since the Aarhus episode. We talk about the end of the C3, take a drive in a very special car, dive into Roubaix and the classics season more generally, visit a very special Preben, talk a little about Seven Coaching Wednesday racing, and of course crack open a few beers along the way. That and much more in this special episode of Lang Distance.

item.description: Episode 100 and the 2026 classics season are dissected in royal surroundings at Hotel Villa in the capital. The Gruppetto is gathered for a big day and an even bigger episode. item.title: S1 Ep692: 6 Things We Learned From the Fastest Paris-Roubaix Ever
item.description: Join the GCN team as they break down a record-breaking weekend at Paris-Roubaix and explore six major lessons from the fastest edition in the race’s history. We also dive into the latest tech news, including the controversy around the new Canyon Endurace and some eye-wateringly expensive titanium mudguards. Finally, we look at how self-driving cars could help cyclists avoid potholes and feature your most ingenious, and questionable, submissions in Hacks & Bodges!

item.title: 413: Wout Wins a Great Edition of Paris-Roubaix
item.description: Wout van Aert did the near-impossible. He beat the two big favorites, Mathieu van der Poel and Tadej Pogacar, in a dramatic edition of Paris-Roubaix that many consider one of the greatest in recent years. Unfortunately, Mads Pedersen had to give up with just over 50 km to go, but he once again put in a strong ride, especially considering his preparations this spring. In the Basque Country, young Paul Seixas held on all the way and became the first Frenchman in 19 years to win a WorldTour stage race. 19 years — which, as we know, is also Paul Sexais’ age... We look ahead to the start of Ardennes Week with Amstel Gold Race, which Mattias Skjelmose won last year. Can the Dane do it again, or will he fall short against favorites like Remco, Tom Pidcock, and others? Veloropa Podcast is presented by betting provider Betano (betano.dk), Danish supplement brand Aioss (get up to DKK 300 off your first orders by using the code veloropa at aioss.dk), and electronics chain Power (avis.power.dk).

item.title: The Most Deserved Victory?
item.description: Wout van Aert finally has his Paris-Roubaix. In one of the most emotional finishes in recent Monument history, the Belgian beat world champion Tadej Pogačar in a two-rider sprint on the Roubaix velodrome, ending the most dominant run of Monument results the sport has ever seen. Bram and Ethan break down every moment: the drama in the Arenberg Forest, Mathieu van der Poel’s race falling apart in an instant with a double puncture and experimental pedals he couldn’t clip into, three punctures and two bike changes for Pogačar, the UAE team car chaos on the cobbles, and what the tactics, or lack of them, from Visma and Alpecin really meant. In the women’s race, Franziska Koch wins for FDJ-Suez in an emotional sprint, as Marianne Vos lines up just days after losing her father. We also look ahead to the Brabantse Pijl — and yes, we do the math on whether Remco can beat Pogačar in a sprint. They discuss the chaos of the day, why so many favorites suffered so many punctures, and what, if anything, Pogačar could have done to turn the tables on Van Aert. item.title: Johan Museeuw Breaks Down the Magic of Paris-Roubaix
item.description: In this episode, three-time Paris-Roubaix winner Johan Museeuw joins Aidan in the Hotseat for an in-depth look at the Hell of the North, breaking down what it really takes to win one of cycling’s most brutal races. Museeuw reflects on how the race has evolved, from the cobbles and equipment of his era to the modern peloton, where riders like Tadej Pogačar and Mathieu van der Poel are redefining what is possible on this terrain. He also explains why experience, positioning, and a bit of luck remain just as decisive as ever. The Belgian icon shares stories from his own victories, including the famous 1996 Roubaix where his team swept the podium, and offers insight into the tactical differences between the cobbled Monuments and how the race can be won or lost long before the velodrome. Looking ahead, Museeuw gives his view on the current favorites, the challenges facing riders like Wout van Aert, and why Paris-Roubaix remains the ultimate test in professional cycling.

item.title: Can Pogačar Crack the Cobbled Code? | Paris-Roubaix Preview
item.description: Spencer Martin and Johan Bruyneel discuss the ongoing Itzulia Basque Country, including what to make of Paul Seixas’ dominance and what it could mean for the future of the sport. They also preview Sunday’s Paris-Roubaix, breaking down what Tadej Pogačar must do to dethrone Mathieu van der Poel as the King of the Cobbles, and how a win would add to his historic dominance in the major one-day races. They also look at who could challenge for the win beyond Van der Poel and Pogačar, and what riders like Mads Pedersen, Wout van Aert, and Filippo Ganna would need to happen to win. The episode covers key tactical scenarios, the importance of positioning in the final cobbled sectors, and how an elite group could stop Pogačar from turning one of cycling’s most iconic races into another solo showcase. item.title: 412: Paris-Roubaix Preview
item.description: On Sunday, the year’s third Monument, Paris-Roubaix, or A Spring Day in Hell as Jørgen Leth famously called it, will be raced. The question is whether Tadej Pogacar can win his fifth Monument in a row and complete the set of all five Monuments. We look at the two big favorites, Pogacar and Mathieu van der Poel, who could win Roubaix for the fourth year in a row, something no one has ever done. We discuss Mads P’s chances, along with the other Danes and outsiders. In the Basque Country, the young French sensation Paul Seixas is dominating everyone, while Mattias Skjelmose took a heavy blow on today’s tough mountain stage. Veloropa Podcast is presented in collaboration with betting provider Betano (betano.dk), the Danish supplement brand Aioss (get up to 300 DKK off your first orders with the code veloropa at aioss.dk), and electronics chain Power (avis.power.dk).

item.description: Are you pushing hard on the bike but still feeling flat, fatigued, or just off? There’s a good chance you’re biking on empty, and you might not even know it. In this episode, I sit down with Kyla Channell, a degreed sports nutritionist, to talk about iron and vitamin D deficiencies, other supplements that are actually worth taking, how to figure out your macro targets, and other nutrition mistakes that may be quietly hurting your performance. 🔥 Get 15% off your first order at Club Ride with coupon code FEMMECYCLIST15. item.title: Paris-Roubaix Preview & Tour of Flanders Debrief
item.description: The Tour of Flanders delivered the race the spring had been building toward. Pogačar, Van der Poel, Van Aert, and Remco Evenepoel all lined up, with Evenepoel making his debut in the race. It was brutal, relentless, and decided, as always, on the Oude Kwaremont. Pogačar won his third Tour of Flanders, making it his 12th Monument overall. Three races this year, three victories. That brings us to Paris-Roubaix. If he wins next Sunday, Pogačar will join Merckx, Van Looy, and De Vlaeminck as the only riders in history to win all five Monuments. That is the big question this week. We’ll dig into Flanders, Evenepoel’s debut, what it means for Van der Poel, and then look ahead to the Hell of the North. This episode is fueled by MNSTRY - 100% vegan sports nutrition trusted by Red Bull-BORA-hansgrohe, CANYON//SRAM zondacrypto, and more. Use code DOMESTIQUE20 at mnstry.com for 20% off your tasting box. Order now!

item.title: 411: Podium Royale in Flanders
item.description: Tadej Pogacar won the Tour of Flanders as expected, and on the podium in Oudenaarde he was joined by world champion colleagues Mathieu van der Poel and Remco Evenepoel, who made a striking debut in the race. Mads P finished a strong fifth, which bodes well for next Sunday’s Paris-Roubaix. We analyze Sunday’s edition of the Tour of Flanders, then look ahead to this week’s Tour of the Basque Country, where Mads P’s teammate Mattias Skjelmose may get a chance to ride for the general classification after Juan Ayuso did not get off to the best start in the opening time trial. We look at the favorites and the demanding route in the northern Spanish stage race, and we also preview Wednesday’s semi-classic for the fast men, Scheldeprijs. Veloropa Podcast is presented by betting provider Betano (betano.dk), the Danish supplement brand Aioss (get up to 300 DKK off your first order with the code veloropa at aioss.dk), and electronics chain Power (avis.power.dk).

item.title: The Big Five
item.description: What a cycling week in Flanders. In today’s episode, Mads speaks openly about his preparation, his respect for Van Aert, how many hours you train in the week leading up to a Monument, how much Klamphugger fuel you burn in the trainer, and how much energy you have left for interviews after seven hours of racing. We also have the Danish rider of the week (can you spell Niklas Larsen?), the crash of the week in the start box, and Jakob firmly insists that Mads is part of “The Big Five.” This episode is brought to you in collaboration with Nitor Energy.

item.title: Tour of Flanders 2026 Breakdown
item.description: Lance Armstrong, George Hincapie, Johan Bruyneel, and Spencer Martin break down Tadej Pogačar's incredible solo win at the Tour of Flanders, which earned him a record-tying third career victory in the race, while the sport's top riders — Mathieu van der Poel, Remco Evenepoel, Wout van Aert, and Mads Pedersen — were left searching for answers behind him. They discuss what, if anything, his rivals could have done, Evenepoel's incredible debut in the race, and, most importantly, whether anyone can stop Pogačar next week at Paris-Roubaix. item.title: 410: Previewing Flanders, Now with Remco
item.description: A high mass is called for on Sunday in Flanders. This year’s edition of De Ronde is just around the corner, and in this episode of Veloropa Podcast you get a rich preview of the race, where Remco Evenepoel has sensationally received a race card from RedBull Bora-Hansgrohe, and where you can hear former Danish winner and classics legend Rolf Sørensen talk about the Tour of Flanders, his 1997 victory, and Kasper Asgreen’s win in 2021. We also look back at a dramatic Dwars door Vlaanderen, where Wout van Aert was caught almost on the finish line by Filippo Ganna, and a Dane with the surname Larsen played a surprising role in the finale. But above all, we look ahead to this year’s second Monument, Sunday’s Tour of Flanders. We examine the big favorites, the route, the tactical possibilities, and not least the Danes. Veloropa Podcast is presented in collaboration with betting provider Betano (betano.dk), the Danish supplement brand Aioss (get up to DKK 300 off with the code veloropa at aioss.dk), and electronics chain Power (avis.power.dk). item.title: Can Anyone Stop Pogačar? | Tour of Flanders Preview
item.description: Spencer Martin and Johan Bruyneel go through Wednesday's Dwars door Vlaanderen showdown between Wout van Aert and Filippo Ganna before previewing Sunday's Tour of Flanders, breaking down how Tadej Pogačar has emerged as the overwhelming favorite after a dominant start to the Classics, and whether anyone — especially Mathieu van der Poel and Van Aert — can realistically challenge him on the race's decisive cobbled climbs. They analyze how Remco Evenepoel's late and unexpected arrival could change the race, discuss the key tactical scenarios, the importance of positioning in the final sequence of climbs, and what it would take to prevent a one-man race in one of cycling's most iconic events. item.description: This episode is fueled by MNSTRY. New customers get 50% off the MNSTRY Tasting Box until Sunday, April 5 — use code MNSTRYWEEK50 at checkout.
